proposals.title: A Stochastic and Time-Dependent Traveling Salesman Problem: Formulations and Adaptive Heuristics for Fleet Routing under Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Traditional routing models assume static, deterministic travel costs, which is unrealistic for applications like postal logistics, urban delivery, and liner shipping where congestion, weather, and service demands cause costs and times to vary dynamically and stochastically. Pre-2026 research on time-dependent and stochastic TSP variants remains fragmented, lacking integrated models that capture both predictable time-of-day variations and random disruptions, limiting their practical utility for fleet operations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an integrated model combining time-dependent travel costs with stochastic cost components can be solved using adaptive, multi-stage heuristics, yielding routing policies that are significantly more robust and cost-effective than those from static or purely deterministic time-dependent models.
proposals.method: We will formulate a two-stage stochastic program with recourse, where the first-stage decides an initial route order, and the second-stage allows dynamic re-sequencing based on realized stochastic costs. Travel times will follow a piecewise linear time-dependent function. Solution methods will combine a Liner Programming-based adaptive large neighborhood search (ALNS) for the first stage with simulation-based policy evaluation. We will develop a rolling horizon heuristic, embedding dynamic programming for real-time adjustments, and benchmark it against static TSP and deterministic time-dependent TSP baselines using historical traffic and demand data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified modeling framework for the TSP with combined time-dependent and stochastic costs, along with a suite of practical heuristic algorithms. It will contribute new benchmark instances and computational insights, directly informing fleet management and service network design under uncertainty in logistics and postal sectors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of predictable time-dependency and random cost fluctuations within a single TSP model, and the development of adaptive heuristics that generate routing policies rather than fixed tours, moving beyond the prevalent static or purely time-dependent deterministic assumptions.

proposals.title: A Matheuristic Framework for Fleet Deployment and Service Allocation in Maritime Logistics under Stochastic Demand
proposals.motivation: Maritime fleet deployment and liner service design are critical for cost efficiency and service reliability, but real-world problems involve large-scale networks and significant demand uncertainty. Pre-2026 exact methods struggle with problem size, while pure heuristics lack optimality guarantees. A principled hybrid approach is needed to deliver robust, near-optimal solutions for practical decision-making in industries like postal and container shipping.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating exact optimization frameworks (e.g., branch-and-price) with adaptive heuristic rules for column generation and primal heuristics will yield solutions of provably high quality for large-scale stochastic fleet problems, outperforming standalone exact or heuristic methods in both computational time and solution robustness.
proposals.method: We will develop a matheuristic based on a two-stage stochastic programming model for fleet deployment. The exact component will be a branch-and-price algorithm using a set-partitioning master problem. Heuristic rules will be embedded to: 1) intelligently generate promising columns (ship routes) via a selective traveling salesman problem (TSP) heuristic with time-dependent profits, and 2) guide the branching strategy and perform rapid feasibility recovery. The framework will be tested on benchmark instances derived from real liner shipping networks.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a new, scalable solution methodology for stochastic fleet management, directly applicable to postal and maritime logistics. It will advance the theory of matheuristics by formally integrating time-dependent profit heuristics within an exact decomposition framework. Practitioners will gain a tool to better balance fleet cost and service level under uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: While matheuristics exist, the novel integration of heuristic rules derived from time-dependent TSP variants into the column generation process of a stochastic fleet model is unexplored. This specifically tailors the hybridization to the structure of maritime allocation problems with service-time constraints.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search and Set Partitioning Approach for the Fleet-Scale Traveling Salesman Problem with Time Windows
proposals.motivation: The Traveling Salesman Problem with Time Windows (TSPTW) is a core model for routing and scheduling in postal, courier, and home service logistics, where customer demand must be met within specific time intervals. Scaling this problem to fleet-level operations, common in real-world applications like postal delivery, introduces complex trade-offs between fleet size, routing cost, and service level adherence. Pre-2026 research often treats fleet composition as fixed or optimizes it separately from detailed routing, missing integrated cost-service analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a solution framework integrating fleet composition decisions with adaptive, time-window-sensitive routing heuristics will yield significantly lower total operational costs while maintaining service guarantees, compared to sequential planning methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-phase matheuristic. First, an Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search (ALNS) will generate a pool of high-quality, feasible routes for individual vehicles, using tailored destroy/repair operators for time window management. Second, a Set Partitioning Problem (SPP) model will select the optimal subset of these routes to cover all demands at minimal total cost, explicitly incorporating heterogeneous vehicle fixed and variable costs. This hybrid ALNS-SPP architecture directly addresses the fleet-scale TSPTW by decoupling route construction from fleet allocation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, practical solution methodology for the integrated fleet-size and routing problem with time windows. It will contribute to the operational research literature by demonstrating how matheuristics can effectively balance strategic fleet costs with tactical routing constraints, offering decision-support tools for logistics managers in postal and service industries.
proposals.novelty: While ALNS and SPP are known separately, their integrated application to simultaneously optimize fleet composition and detailed TSPTW routes is novel. The approach explicitly bridges the gap between Laporte's recent work on fleet operations under uncertainty and selective, time-dependent routing problems.

proposals.title: A Fleet Size and Mix Optimization Model for Time-Dependent Routing with Stochastic Demand
proposals.motivation: In logistics and postal operations, the assumption of a homogeneous vehicle fleet is often unrealistic and leads to suboptimal resource allocation and higher costs. Real-world fleets comprise vehicles with varied capacities, fixed costs, and variable operating costs. Optimizing the deployment of such a heterogeneous fleet, especially under uncertain daily demand and time-dependent travel times (e.g., urban congestion), is a critical but underexplored problem in operations research with significant practical implications for cost reduction and service level improvement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling fleet heterogeneity within a stochastic and time-dependent routing framework will yield significant cost savings over models assuming fleet homogeneity or treating uncertainty and time-dependence separately. The optimal fleet composition will be highly sensitive to the variance of demand and the structure of time windows.
proposals.method: We will formulate a two-stage stochastic programming model. The first stage decides the fleet composition (number of vehicles of each type). The second stage, for each demand scenario, solves a time-dependent vehicle routing problem (TDVRP) to allocate customers and routes to the available heterogeneous fleet. To solve this complex problem, we will develop a matheuristic combining a metaheuristic (e.g., Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search) to explore routing solutions with embedded mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) solved via a commercial solver (e.g., CPLEX or Gurobi) for the strategic fleet mix decisions. Sample Average Approximation will be used to handle the stochastic demand.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel integrated optimization model and a scalable solution methodology for a fundamental logistics problem. It will provide decision-support tools for strategic fleet acquisition and tactical daily routing under realistic conditions, directly extending Laporte's work on fleet operations under uncertainty and time-dependent profits. The computational study will quantify the value of fleet heterogeneity and integrated planning.
proposals.novelty: While heterogeneous fleet VRP and stochastic VRP exist, their integration with time-dependent travel times and a two-stage model that jointly optimizes strategic fleet mix and operational routing under uncertainty is novel. This bridges the gap between strategic asset allocation and operational execution in a unified stochastic and dynamic framework.

proposals.title: A Multi-Depot Fleet Deployment and Routing Model for Liner Shipping Under Stochastic Demand
proposals.motivation: Modern liner shipping and postal logistics increasingly rely on distributed, multi-hub networks to enhance service coverage and resilience. Existing fleet deployment and routing models, such as those in Laporte's recent work on ship fleets under uncertainty, often assume a single depot, limiting their applicability to these complex, real-world networks. Extending these models to multiple depots is crucial for optimizing asset allocation and routing in large-scale, decentralized systems where demand is stochastic and service-level agreements must be met cost-effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating and solving a stochastic, multi-depot fleet deployment and routing problem will yield significantly lower total expected costs and more robust operational plans compared to applying single-depot models to decentralized networks, without compromising service-level constraints.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage stochastic programming model. The first stage decides the initial allocation of a heterogeneous fleet to multiple depots. The second stage, for each demand scenario, solves a multi-depot vehicle routing problem with time windows (MDVRPTW) to fulfill stochastic customer demand. Solution approaches will combine a Branch-and-Cut framework for the first-stage allocation with adaptive large neighborhood search (ALNS) metaheuristics, enhanced by scenario decomposition techniques, to solve the second-stage routing problems efficiently.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated optimization model for joint fleet allocation and routing under uncertainty in multi-depot liner and postal logistics networks. It will contribute a new benchmark suite based on realistic maritime and parcel distribution data and demonstrate quantifiable benefits of a multi-depot perspective over a centralized one for service-sensitive operations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of stochastic multi-depot fleet allocation with detailed time-sensitive routing, a combination not addressed in Laporte's prior single-depot fleet work or the selective TSP with time-dependent profits, moving from a centralized to a truly network-centric operational view.

proposals.title: Large-Scale Validation of a Selective Traveling Salesman Algorithm for Time-Sensitive Freight Distribution
proposals.motivation: In freight logistics, carriers often face the selective traveling salesman problem (STSP) where serving all customers is infeasible or unprofitable, requiring selection based on time-dependent profits and constraints. Real-world networks, such as postal or less-than-truckload distribution, involve hundreds of nodes and complex time windows, but existing solution methods are often validated only on small or synthetic instances. A rigorous test on large, real-world networks is needed to prove practical viability and scalability.
proposals.hypothesis: The proposed algorithm for the Selective Traveling Salesman Problem with Time-Dependent Profits can be scaled efficiently to solve large, real-world freight distribution instances while maintaining solution quality and computational tractability.
proposals.method: We will adapt the exact or advanced heuristic algorithm from Laporte's prior STSP work, integrating it within a matheuristic framework combining integer programming and local search. The method will be tested on newly constructed large-scale case studies derived from real postal service delivery networks and multi-depot freight carrier data, featuring up to 500 nodes, time-dependent service profits, and hard time windows. Computational experiments will benchmark performance against state-of-the-art solvers and heuristic methods from pre-2026 literature, using metrics of solution quality, runtime, and robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, empirical assessment of the algorithm's scalability and practical utility, offering practitioners a validated tool for complex routing and selection decisions. It will extend the theoretical analysis of the STSP to a significant applied context, generating publicly available benchmark instances for future research.
proposals.novelty: While the core algorithm exists, its application and rigorous validation at the scale and complexity of actual national postal or freight networks, with real operational data, represents a novel and significant step beyond typical academic benchmarks.

proposals.title: A Bi-Level Optimization Framework for Designing Emission-Reducing Transport Policies: Speed Limits and Tolls in Road Freight Networks
proposals.motivation: The road freight sector is a major contributor to greenhouse gas emissions, with fuel consumption highly sensitive to vehicle speed and routing choices. While carriers optimize for their own costs, resulting routes and speeds are often not environmentally optimal. Regulatory bodies lack analytical tools to design effective policies, such as network-wide speed limits or congestion tolls, that can align carrier self-interest with system-wide emission reduction targets in a predictable manner.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bi-level optimization model, where the upper level sets network-wide policies and the lower level represents the aggregated routing and speed decisions of profit-maximizing carriers, can identify Pareto-efficient policy instruments that significantly reduce emissions with minimal impact on overall logistics service levels.
proposals.method: We will formulate a mathematical program with equilibrium constraints (MPEC). The lower level will model carrier behavior as a time-dependent vehicle routing problem (VRP) with speed optimization, incorporating fuel consumption models from recent liner shipping research. The upper level will optimize policy parameters (e.g., link-specific speed limits or tolls) to minimize system emissions. Solution approaches will leverage decomposition techniques (e.g., column generation) and heuristic frameworks (e.g., iterative penalty methods) inspired by Laporte's work on fleet operations under uncertainty and the selective TSP, adapting them to this inverse policy design context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative decision-support tool for transport and environmental regulators. It will advance the field of sustainable logistics by formally linking policy design to carrier operational models, generating actionable policy bundles. The methodology will extend bi-level optimization techniques to a new class of environmental policy problems in freight transport.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the traditional carrier-centric optimization to solve for the regulatory policy inputs, and in jointly optimizing speed limits and tolls within a unified bi-level framework that explicitly models carriers' time-dependent routing and speed adjustments, a synthesis not found in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Bilevel Optimization Model for Fleet Routing to Maximize Served Demand Under a Hard Carbon Emission Cap
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 context of tightening environmental regulations and corporate carbon accounting, fleet operators face the dual challenge of maintaining service levels while adhering to strict emission budgets. Traditional vehicle routing problems (VRPs) often treat emissions as a cost to minimize, not as a hard constraint, which is insufficient for compliance scenarios. This research addresses the critical operational question of how to prioritize and serve customer demand when total route emissions are capped, a problem relevant to postal, logistics, and liner shipping networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating the problem as a bilevel optimization—where the upper level allocates an emission budget to routes and the lower level solves a profit-maximizing selective routing problem—we can develop effective exact and heuristic methods that identify Pareto-efficient frontiers of service level versus emissions.
proposals.method: We will develop a mathematical model integrating a time- and load-dependent emission function within a Selective Traveling Salesman Problem (STSP) or Prize-Collecting VRP framework. The core solution approach will involve a branch-and-cut algorithm for exact solutions on moderate instances, enhanced by adaptive large neighborhood search (ALNS) heuristics for larger-scale problems, drawing on Laporte's expertise in metaheuristics and exact methods for routing under uncertainty. The emission cap will be enforced via constraint generation or integrated into the ALNS destroy/repair operators.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, compliance-focused variant to the VRP literature, specifically the class of 'green VRPs'. It will provide practitioners with a decision-support tool for emission-constrained fleet planning. The methodological contribution lies in adapting bilevel and selective routing techniques to the novel objective of demand/service maximization under an emission resource constraint.
proposals.novelty: The novelty stems from inverting the typical 'minimize cost/emissions for all demand' paradigm to 'maximize demand for a fixed emission budget', creating a dual perspective to standard green VRPs. Furthermore, integrating time-dependent profits (from recent STSP work) with a hard emission cap creates a rich, realistic trade-off model not extensively studied pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Multi-Depot Fleet Management Model for City-Wide Courier and Postal Operations under Demand Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: The rapid growth of e-commerce and same-day delivery services before 2026 places immense pressure on urban logistics systems. Operating a large fleet from a single depot is inefficient for metropolitan-scale operations, leading to high costs and poor service levels. A multi-depot strategy is essential, but existing models often lack integration with realistic, time-dependent demand uncertainty and complex urban constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tailored two-stage stochastic programming model, combined with a metaheuristic solution framework, can yield robust, cost-effective multi-depot fleet allocation and routing plans that significantly improve service reliability and reduce operational costs compared to single-depot or deterministic approaches.
proposals.method: We will formulate a two-stage stochastic program where first-stage decisions allocate vehicles to depots, and second-stage recourse decisions solve time-dependent multi-depot vehicle routing problems under sampled demand scenarios. The core solution architecture will combine a Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) metaheuristic, informed by Laporte's work on the selective TSP, with a liner programming-based cost and fleet sizing module. This hybrid approach will manage the computational complexity of scaling to city-wide instances.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified methodological framework for large-scale, multi-depot fleet management under uncertainty, directly applicable to postal and courier services. It will contribute new benchmark instances and algorithmic strategies for a class of problems at the intersection of fleet allocation, routing, and stochastic optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated treatment of strategic fleet allocation to multiple depots with operational, time-dependent routing and recourse actions under uncertainty, a combination not fully addressed in pre-2026 literature which often treats these problems separately or with simplified demand models.

proposals.title: Inverse Fleet Sizing and Mix Optimization Under Service-Level Constraints: A Stochastic Programming Approach
proposals.motivation: Traditional fleet planning models often treat service level as an output or a constraint to be minimized against cost. In practice, logistics and postal operators (e.g., Canada Post) frequently face the inverse problem: given a mandated service level (e.g., next-day delivery guarantee) and a stochastic demand forecast, what is the minimal-cost fleet composition? This problem is critical for strategic planning under regulatory or competitive service agreements, yet lacks dedicated methodological frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-stage stochastic integer programming model, incorporating time-dependent demand uncertainty and explicit service-level constraints, can be solved efficiently to determine a robust, cost-optimal fleet size and mix that guarantees a predefined service probability.
proposals.method: We will formulate a two-stage stochastic integer program. The first-stage decides the fleet composition (size and mix of vehicle types). The second-stage, for each demand scenario, solves a tactical allocation and routing problem to meet demand subject to the fleet availability, with penalties for unmet service. The service-level constraint will be enforced as a chance constraint or a conditional value-at-risk (CVaR) constraint on the proportion of demand served. Solution methods will leverage Benders decomposition, enhanced with combinatorial cuts, and heuristic frameworks (e.g., adaptive large neighborhood search) to handle the complex second-stage routing subproblems, building on Laporte's work on fleet operations under uncertainty and time-dependent routing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel optimization model and solution methodology for the inverse fleet planning problem. It will contribute a practical decision-support tool for strategic planners in postal and logistics sectors, explicitly linking service-level targets to capital investment in fleet assets. The research will also advance stochastic programming techniques for combined fleet sizing and routing problems.
proposals.novelty: While fleet sizing and vehicle routing problems are well-studied, the explicit inversion—optimizing the fixed assets *given* a service-level target as an input—is a non-obvious and under-explored perspective. The integration of a hard service-level constraint within a stochastic fleet composition model, solved via decomposition, moves beyond standard cost-service trade-off analyses.

proposals.title: A Multi-Stage Stochastic Programming Model for Fleet Deployment and Routing with Dynamic Demand and Multi-Period Recourse
proposals.motivation: In maritime logistics and postal operations, fleet management decisions are inherently sequential and must adapt to evolving demand and operational uncertainties over time. Existing two-stage stochastic models are limited, as they cannot adequately capture the value of multiple, periodic recourse actions (e.g., re-routing, chartering, speed adjustments) throughout a planning horizon. A multi-stage framework is essential for realistic, cost-effective planning under uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating fleet deployment and routing as a multi-stage stochastic program with more than two decision periods will yield significantly lower expected operational costs and more robust policies compared to traditional two-stage or deterministic models, by better exploiting the dynamic evolution of information.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-stage stochastic integer programming model, building upon Laporte's prior work on ship fleet uncertainty and time-dependent profits. The model will incorporate a scenario tree representing the evolution of stochastic demand over multiple periods. To solve it, we will employ a progressive hedging algorithm (PHA) or a multi-stage Benders decomposition, tailored to handle the combinatorial routing structure. Computational experiments will be conducted using modified instances from maritime and postal logistics literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel modeling framework for dynamic fleet management under uncertainty, extending the state-of-the-art beyond two-stage formulations. It will deliver algorithmic strategies for solving such complex multi-period problems and quantify the value of multi-stage adaptive planning in practical logistics settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a multi-stage stochastic programming structure with combinatorial fleet deployment and routing decisions, moving beyond the prevalent two-stage paradigm in this application domain to enable sequential, periodic recourse.

proposals.title: A Stochastic and Time-Dependent Fleet Relocation Model for Postal and Parcel Service Networks
proposals.motivation: In postal and express parcel delivery, operational decisions on facility location and vehicle relocation are typically based on deterministic travel times. This assumption is unrealistic in urban environments where congestion causes significant variability, leading to suboptimal resource allocation, increased costs, and degraded service reliability. Integrating realistic travel time dynamics is crucial for building robust logistics systems that can meet stringent service-level agreements under uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling travel times as either stochastic variables or explicit time-dependent functions within facility location and fleet relocation models will yield solutions that are significantly more cost-effective and service-robust than those derived from deterministic models, when evaluated under real-world variable conditions.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage stochastic programming model with recourse, where first-stage decisions involve strategic facility location, and second-stage recourse actions handle daily fleet relocation and routing under realized travel times. Travel time uncertainty will be modeled via scenarios generated from historical GPS data. For the time-dependent variant, we will use piecewise linear speed profiles. Solution approaches will leverage L-shaped decomposition and heuristic frameworks (e.g., adaptive large neighborhood search) tailored to handle the integrated location-routing problem structure, building on Laporte's expertise in liner shipping and selective TSP models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, integrated modeling framework for location-relocation problems under travel time uncertainty, directly applicable to postal and urban logistics. It will deliver new algorithmic strategies for this challenging class of two-stage stochastic programs and quantify the value of stochastic solutions (VSS) in a practical context, offering managers clear evidence for adopting advanced planning tools.
proposals.novelty: While stochastic vehicle routing is well-studied, its integration with strategic facility location and tactical fleet relocation under time-dependent or stochastic travel times remains largely unexplored, particularly for service network design with profit or demand fulfillment objectives, as seen in selective TSPs.

proposals.title: A Unified Taxonomy and Systematic Review for Multi-Depot Vehicle Routing with Dynamic Network Elements
proposals.motivation: Modern logistics, from postal delivery to liner shipping, increasingly involves operations from multiple depots within networks subject to dynamic changes, such as mobile depots (e.g., drones from trucks) or infrastructure disruptions. The literature is fragmented, with studies on multi-depot vehicle routing problems (MDVRP) and dynamic/adaptive routing largely developed in parallel. A structured framework is needed to unify these concepts, map the solution space, and guide future research towards high-impact, realistic problem settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic taxonomy based on depot mobility (fixed, temporary, mobile) and network dynamism (traffic, demand, connectivity) will reveal significant, high-potential research gaps at their intersection, particularly for problems combining strategic depot allocation with operational routing under uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will first develop a multi-dimensional taxonomy integrating attributes from classical MDVRP, dynamic VRP, and location-routing literature. This taxonomy will be used to code and analyze a comprehensive corpus of pre-2026 publications. The analysis will employ gap analysis techniques, quantifying coverage and identifying unexplored combinations. Promising gaps will be evaluated for theoretical challenge and practical relevance, drawing on metrics from cost-service trade-off analyses common in fleet and liner operations research.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified classification and state-of-the-art review for this complex problem domain. It will yield a publicly available research roadmap, prioritizing specific problem configurations for future algorithmic development. This will help focus the field's efforts on the most synergistic extensions of classical models.
proposals.novelty: While taxonomies exist for static VRP variants, none systematically integrate the dimensions of depot multiplicity and network dynamism. The gap analysis will move beyond descriptive review to proactively identify and rank underexplored, high-value problem classes for future investigation.

proposals.title: A Comparative Analysis of Solution Methods for Electric Vehicle Swapping Station Location Problems: A Meta-Study on Fleet and Demand Characteristics
proposals.motivation: The strategic location of Electric Vehicle Swapping Stations (ESS) is critical for supporting fleet electrification and meeting growing demand, directly impacting operational cost and service level. While numerous solution techniques exist—from exact methods like integer linear programming to heuristics and metaheuristics—there is no systematic guidance on which method is most efficient for different ESS problem classes defined by fleet size, demand patterns, and network constraints. This gap leads to inefficient planning and suboptimal infrastructure investments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative efficiency of exact, heuristic, and matheuristic solution methods for ESS location problems is not uniform but is systematically determined by problem characteristics such as fleet composition, demand time-dependency, and the presence of service-level constraints.
proposals.method: We will design a comprehensive computational study based on pre-2026 literature. First, we will formalize distinct ESS problem classes (e.g., static deterministic, time-dependent, stochastic demand) inspired by Laporte's work on fleet operations and time-dependent routing. For each class, we will implement and benchmark a suite of methods: exact solvers for mixed-integer linear programs, tailored construction and local search heuristics, and matheuristics combining linear programming with metaheuristic frameworks. Performance will be evaluated on standardized metrics of solution quality, computational time, and scalability using both synthetic and real-world postal/logistics network data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, evidence-based guide for practitioners and researchers to select the most appropriate solution technique for a given ESS planning context. It will advance the field of sustainable logistics by creating a methodological taxonomy that links problem structure to algorithmic performance, thereby improving planning efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While comparative studies exist for generic facility location, a focused meta-analysis on ESS problems that integrates fleet operational constraints, time-dependent demand, and a broad range of contemporary (pre-2026) solution architectures is novel. The synthesis of Laporte's expertise in fleet management, routing, and selective TSPs within a location analysis framework is a unique contribution.

proposals.title: Robust Fleet Deployment and Routing for Postal and Liner Shipping under Demand and Travel Time Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: In postal and liner shipping operations, effective fleet management is critically undermined by uncertainty in customer demand and port-to-port travel times, which are exacerbated by weather and congestion. Traditional stochastic programming approaches often rely on precise probability distributions that are difficult to estimate accurately in practice, leading to plans that are fragile in real-world conditions. This research addresses the need for decision-support tools that provide reliable, cost-effective service guarantees despite this deep uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying robust optimization techniques, specifically budget-of-uncertainty and adjustable robust optimization frameworks from finance, will yield fleet deployment and routing solutions for postal networks that are both less conservative than worst-case analysis and more reliably feasible under realized uncertainties than stochastic models with misspecified distributions.
proposals.method: We will formulate a two-stage robust optimization model integrating fleet allocation (first-stage) and demand fulfillment routing (second-stage). The uncertainty sets for daily demand and arc travel times will be constructed using polyhedral budgets of uncertainty, informed by historical data. The resulting model will be solved using a column-and-constraint generation (C&CG) algorithm, decomposing the problem into a master problem (fleet deployment) and adversarial separation subproblems. Computational experiments will be conducted on benchmark instances derived from real-world postal and liner shipping networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, tractable modeling framework for robust maritime and postal logistics under concurrent demand and travel time uncertainty. It will provide practitioners with a methodology to explicitly trade off cost efficiency with operational robustness. The computational study will deliver managerial insights on how robustness budgets impact fleet size, routing patterns, and service levels.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tailored integration of finance-derived robust optimization techniques into the combined fleet deployment and routing problem for postal/liner shipping, a domain where such methods were not yet fully explored pre-2026. It specifically addresses the interplay of two key uncertainties (demand and time) within a unified adjustable robust framework for this application.

proposals.title: A Dual Optimization Model for Minimum Fleet Sizing with Probabilistic Coverage Constraints in Emergency Medical Services
proposals.motivation: Emergency Medical Services (EMS) face the critical challenge of balancing high-quality coverage with operational costs. Traditional location-allocation models often fix the fleet size and optimize coverage or response time. The dual problem—minimizing the fleet given a mandatory coverage standard—is crucial for cost-effective public service planning, especially for municipal agencies with strict budgetary and performance targets. This directly addresses a core resource allocation problem in public logistics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating the ambulance fleet minimization problem as a dual covering model with probabilistic response-time constraints, we can determine a provably minimal fleet size that meets predefined service-level agreements, and that this minimum is sensitive to the spatial-temporal distribution of demand and network travel times.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage stochastic integer programming model. The first stage decides the number and base locations of ambulances. The second stage, using a set of historical demand scenarios, evaluates coverage via a hypercube queuing model or a maximum expected covering location problem (MEXCLP) approximation to account for ambulance busy periods. Solution approaches will combine exact methods (e.g., branch-and-cut) for smaller instances with metaheuristics (e.g, adaptive large neighborhood search) for larger, realistic networks, drawing on Laporte's expertise in fleet and routing under uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous optimization framework for the fleet-sizing dual of coverage models, contributing a new tool for EMS planners. It will yield insights into the trade-off between fleet size and probabilistic coverage reliability. The methodology will also be applicable to other fleet-based services with coverage mandates, such as police or fire departments.
proposals.novelty: While coverage models are well-studied, the explicit minimization of fleet size as the primary objective under probabilistic service-level constraints represents a shift in perspective. The novelty lies in integrating stochastic queuing-based coverage evaluation directly into a fleet-minimization optimization model, rather than treating it as a post-hoc analysis.

proposals.title: A Collaborative Optimization Framework for Large-Scale Carrier Coalitions in Digital Freight Marketplaces
proposals.motivation: The rise of digital freight platforms enables unprecedented collaboration between independent carriers, but existing operational research models for fleet management (e.g., vehicle routing, liner shipping) are designed for single, centralized fleets. Scaling collaboration to large coalitions sharing jobs via a common marketplace introduces complex, large-scale problems of demand allocation, cost-sharing, and coordinated routing under uncertainty, which are not adequately addressed by pre-2026 literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical optimization framework, combining a cooperative game-theoretic mechanism for stable coalition formation and profit allocation with a decentralized, matheuristic for real-time routing, can achieve significant systemic cost reductions and service level improvements for large-scale collaborative fleets.
proposals.method: The method will first model the problem as a two-stage stochastic program to handle demand uncertainty. For the coalition formation and job allocation stage, we will adapt the Shapley value and core concepts from cooperative game theory, using approximation algorithms for computational tractability. For the operational routing stage, we will develop a large neighborhood search (LNS) metaheuristic, decomposed by carrier but coordinated through the marketplace's central platform to solve the resulting multi-fleet vehicle routing problem with time windows (VRPTW).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated optimization model and solution methodology tailored for large-scale, multi-carrier digital marketplaces. It will contribute new insights into the trade-offs between collaboration benefits and coordination complexity, and deliver a practical decision-support tool for platform operators.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of coalitional game theory with large-scale, decentralized routing heuristics within a single digital marketplace framework, moving beyond traditional single-fleet or small-coalition models to address the true scale and incentive structures of platform-based logistics.

proposals.title: A Stochastic Programming Approach for an Autonomous Owner-Operator: Balancing Profit, Workload, and Demand Uncertainty in a Single-Vehicle Routing Problem
proposals.motivation: The rise of autonomous delivery vehicles and the gig economy model creates a new class of operational problems: the single owner-operator managing one asset (e.g., a drone, a small autonomous truck, or a human-driven vehicle under strict personal constraints). Pre-2026 research on fleet management under uncertainty (e.g., ship fleets) and selective routing (e.g., the Traveling Salesman Problem with Profits) does not address the unique constraints of a single operator, who must balance daily profit targets, strict personal workload limits (e.g., driving hours), and stochastic customer demand to ensure sustainable operations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-stage stochastic programming model with recourse, incorporating hard constraints on daily profit and operator workload, can yield a robust daily policy that significantly outperforms deterministic or myopic strategies in meeting the operator's long-term viability goals under demand uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will formulate a Two-Stage Stochastic Integer Program. The first stage decides which customers to commit to serving (a selective routing 'seed' based on expected profit and location), akin to a time-dependent profit TSP. The second stage models recourse actions for revealed stochastic demand, such as accepting or rejecting newly revealed requests within the remaining workload window. We will solve this using a Sample Average Approximation (SAA) framework combined with a tailored branch-and-cut algorithm, leveraging Laporte's expertise in exact and heuristic methods for routing and scheduling. The model's cost and service parameters will be grounded in realistic postal/parcel delivery contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel operational model bridging the gap between large-scale fleet logistics and micro-entrepreneurial operations. It will provide a formal decision-support framework for autonomous or single-vehicle operators facing uncertainty. The methodology will advance stochastic routing literature by integrating hard personal constraints (profit and time) as first-class model components rather than secondary objectives.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling down sophisticated fleet-level stochastic optimization concepts to a single, tightly constrained agent, explicitly modeling the operator's profit sustainability and personal workload limits as inflexible constraints within a stochastic programming framework, a perspective largely absent in pre-2026 vehicle routing literature.

proposals.title: A System-Optimal Model for Multi-Echelon Global Supply Chain Design with Primary Emissions Minimization
proposals.motivation: Global supply chains are major contributors to greenhouse gas emissions, yet traditional optimization models treat cost as the primary objective, relegating environmental impact to a secondary constraint or penalty. This cost-primary paradigm can obscure holistic reconfigurations essential for deep decarbonization, such as strategic nearshoring or modal shifts, which may be economically suboptimal but environmentally superior. With increasing regulatory pressure (e.g., EU ETS) and corporate net-zero commitments pre-2026, there is an urgent need for decision-support tools that explicitly prioritize planetary boundaries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inverting the primary objective to minimize total lifecycle emissions across a fully integrated, multi-echelon network will reveal system-optimal configurations—involving trade-offs between transportation, production, and inventory—that are Pareto-efficient and remain invisible to cost-primary models.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-objective mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model integrating facility location, production allocation, multi-modal routing (maritime, air, rail, road), and inventory policies. The core will be a multi-commodity, multi-period network flow formulation, extending Laporte's work on fleet operations under uncertainty and selective TSP variants. Emissions factors (CO2e, pollutants) will be embedded as primary objective coefficients. Solution methods will combine exact algorithms (e.g., branch-and-cut) for smaller instances with metaheuristics (e.g., adaptive large neighborhood search) and simulation for scalable, stochastic analysis of global networks.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel methodological framework for supply chain design where environmental impact is the principal driver, directly contributing to the OR-for-climate literature. It will yield a practical tool for generating Pareto frontiers between emissions and cost, enabling managers and policymakers to evaluate trade-offs for deep decarbonization strategies. The work will also advance the integration of multi-echelon inventory-routing problems with strategic facility location under a primary non-economic objective.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the systemic inversion of the primary objective from cost to emissions across the entire supply chain echelon, forcing a re-evaluation of optimal network structures. This moves beyond simply adding an emissions constraint or performing post-hoc analysis, enabling the discovery of configurations that are fundamentally shaped by environmental imperatives.

proposals.title: A Multi-Objective Fleet Composition and Routing Model for Heterogeneous Vehicles with Alternative Fuels
proposals.motivation: The logistics and postal sectors face increasing pressure to reduce emissions and operational costs simultaneously. Pre-2026 research on green vehicle routing often assumes homogeneous fleets or a single alternative fuel, which does not reflect the reality of companies transitioning their fleets with a mix of conventional, electric, hybrid, and other low-emission vehicles. This gap limits the practical applicability of routing models for strategic fleet management under environmental targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling heterogeneous vehicle fleets with different fuel types, associated refueling/recharging infrastructure constraints, and time-dependent energy costs within a unified routing and fleet allocation framework will yield significantly different and more cost-effective operational strategies than models assuming fleet homogeneity.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-objective mathematical model combining fleet composition (investment/selection) and operational routing decisions. The model will incorporate vehicle-specific attributes: purchase cost, fuel/energy consumption (potentially time- or load-dependent), range, and access to refueling stations. Solution methods will leverage Laporte's expertise in liner shipping and fleet uncertainty: a metaheuristic framework (e.g., adaptive large neighborhood search) will be designed to handle the combinatorial complexity, integrating specialized operators for vehicle assignment and route sequencing, inspired by techniques for the selective TSP and ship fleet management under uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, integrated optimization model for the strategic-operational planning of mixed fleets, providing decision-support tools for logistics managers. It will advance the field of environmental routing by moving beyond fixed, homogeneous fleets to offer actionable insights on optimal fleet transition paths and daily operations, balancing cost and service requirements.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous optimization of heterogeneous vehicle selection and multi-period routing under realistic fueling constraints, bridging the gap between strategic fleet composition models and tactical green vehicle routing problems, which are typically studied in isolation.

proposals.title: A Taxonomy for Green Vehicle Routing: Problem Structures, Objectives, and Constraints
proposals.motivation: By 2025, the operational research community has produced a vast but fragmented literature on vehicle routing problems (VRPs) incorporating environmental objectives, such as minimizing emissions, fuel consumption, or noise. This proliferation of models—like Pollution-Routing Problems, Green VRPs, and Energy-Minimizing VRPs—lacks a unifying framework, hindering comparative analysis, benchmarking, and the identification of core methodological challenges. A systematic taxonomy is urgently needed to structure this field and guide future research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that environmental VRPs can be systematically classified along a set of fundamental dimensions related to their objective functions, constraint structures, and underlying operational contexts, revealing distinct clusters of problem types that share core methodological properties.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comprehensive literature review of pre-2026 work to extract key problem features. The taxonomy will be developed along dimensions such as: (1) primary environmental objective (e.g., fuel, CO2, noise), (2) cost-service trade-off structure (e.g., bi-objective, monetized externalities), (3) time-dependency and uncertainty modeling (linking to Laporte's work on fleet operations under uncertainty), and (4) technological fleet context (e.g., conventional, hybrid, electric). The classification will be validated by mapping seminal problems (e.g., the Time-Dependent Pollution-Routing Problem) and analyzing solution method applicability across categories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, structured taxonomy for environmental routing, offering researchers a clear map of the field. It will identify critical gaps in existing literature and suggest promising directions for methodological development, particularly in integrating uncertainty and multi-objective optimization. The taxonomy will also serve as a practical guide for practitioners selecting appropriate models for specific green logistics applications.
proposals.novelty: While taxonomies exist for classical VRPs, none systematically deconstruct the specific and complex interplay of environmental objectives, real-world constraints (like time-dependent speeds), and emerging fleet technologies that define this modern problem class. Our approach integrates these aspects into a single analytical framework.

proposals.title: A Closed-Loop, Data-Assimilative Framework for Robust Green Fleet Routing Under Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Green vehicle routing models often rely on static, a priori estimates of energy consumption and emissions, which fail under real-world traffic, weather, and demand volatility. This simulation-to-reality gap undermines the promised environmental benefits of optimized plans. A principled methodology is needed to dynamically calibrate eco-routing models with live data, ensuring robust performance in uncertain operational contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating real-time data assimilation with stochastic combinatorial optimization will produce vehicle routes and schedules that maintain significantly higher eco-efficiency and service reliability under unpredictable conditions compared to static or myopic adaptive approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer framework. First, a data assimilation module will continuously update a stochastic energy consumption model using live telemetry (GPS, fuel flow) and exogenous data (traffic, weather). Second, this calibrated model will drive a robust optimization core, formulated as a Time-Dependent Pollution-Routing Problem (PRP) with stochastic demands and travel times. We will solve it using a hybrid metaheuristic (e.g., Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search) combined with sample-based stochastic programming techniques, enabling closed-loop re-planning.
proposals.contribution: The research will yield a novel, generalizable methodology for robust green logistics that demonstrably closes the simulation-to-reality gap. It will provide new algorithmic insights for integrating data assimilation into combinatorial optimization and deliver validated strategies for maintaining eco-efficiency under uncertainty, directly applicable to postal and liner shipping fleets.
proposals.novelty: The closed-loop integration of continuous model calibration via data assimilation within a stochastic combinatorial optimization framework for green routing is a novel synthesis beyond existing adaptive or robust-only models. It moves from open-loop robust planning to a truly self-correcting operational system.

proposals.title: A Column Generation Framework for Large-Scale Maritime Fleet Deployment with Environmental Constraints
proposals.motivation: The maritime shipping industry faces increasing pressure to reduce its environmental footprint, including emissions and fuel consumption, while maintaining cost-effective and reliable service. Pre-2026, large-scale fleet deployment and routing problems under such constraints are computationally challenging, often requiring simplifications that limit practical applicability. This research addresses the need for exact or near-exact optimization methods that can handle the scale and complexity of real-world liner shipping networks with integrated environmental regulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a column generation approach, embedded within a branch-and-price framework, can efficiently solve large-scale, environmentally constrained fleet deployment and routing problems by decomposing the problem and dynamically generating profitable ship routes, leading to superior solutions compared to monolithic models or heuristics.
proposals.method: The core method is a tailored branch-and-price algorithm. The master problem will be a set partitioning/covering formulation for assigning vessels to services, minimizing total cost (including fuel and emission penalties). The pricing subproblems, one per ship type or group, will be resource-constrained shortest path problems on time-space networks, generating feasible routes with associated cost, time, and emission profiles. These subproblems will be solved using dynamic programming with state variables for time, cumulative demand, and emissions. The framework will be tested on benchmark instances derived from real liner shipping networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable exact solution methodology for a critical class of problems in green logistics. It will yield new insights into the trade-offs between operational cost, service level, and environmental impact in fleet management. The resulting software framework and benchmark solutions will serve as a valuable tool for both researchers and practitioners in maritime operations research.
proposals.novelty: While column generation is known for vehicle routing, its application to integrated fleet deployment and routing under explicit, hard environmental constraints (e.g., emission caps per leg or voyage) in liner shipping is novel. The innovation lies in the specific modeling of time-dependent environmental costs within the pricing subproblems, directly linking operational decisions to regulatory compliance.

proposals.title: A Multi-Objective Fleet Allocation and Routing Model for City-Wide Green Logistics Under Zonal Access Regulations
proposals.motivation: Urban logistics faces growing pressure from environmental regulations, such as low-emission zones and time-window restrictions, which complicate fleet management and routing. Existing models often focus on single companies or vehicle types, lacking a holistic, city-wide perspective that integrates heterogeneous fleets (e.g., electric, cargo bikes, conventional vans) under shared infrastructure and regulatory constraints. This gap hinders the systemic optimization needed for sustainable urban freight.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-stage stochastic programming model, integrating strategic fleet allocation with operational adaptive routing, can significantly reduce total system costs and emissions for a city-wide logistics network compared to decentralized, myopic planning, even under uncertain demand and regulatory enforcement.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage model. The first stage decides the strategic allocation of a heterogeneous fleet (electric vehicles, cargo bikes, etc.) to depots, considering capital and environmental costs. The second stage is a stochastic adaptive routing problem, modeled as a time-dependent, selective traveling salesman problem with profits, where 'profit' is derived from serving demand while penalizing emissions and access violations. Solution methods will combine large neighborhood search (for routing) with integer L-shaped decomposition or sample average approximation (for the stochastic program), leveraging insights from Laporte's prior work on fleet operations under uncertainty and time-dependent profits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide an integrated optimization framework for public and private stakeholders to plan green urban logistics systems. It will advance methodological knowledge by combining stochastic fleet allocation with adaptive, time-dependent routing in a novel way. The model will yield actionable insights into optimal fleet mix and depot placement under regulatory scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the city-wide scale integrating multiple carriers' operations under shared regulations, and the explicit coupling of strategic green fleet allocation with operational adaptive routing under demand and regulatory uncertainty, extending Laporte's selective TSP with time-dependent profits to a system-level, multi-objective context.

proposals.title: Inverse Policy Design for Green Vehicle Routing: An Evolutionary Multi-Objective Framework
proposals.motivation: Governments face the complex challenge of designing environmental policies (e.g., carbon taxes, low-emission zones) to reduce transportation emissions without crippling logistics operations. Current policy analysis often relies on static models or ex-post evaluations, failing to account for the dynamic, operational trade-offs faced by fleet managers under uncertainty. A proactive, data-driven method is needed to design policies that are both environmentally effective and economically viable for operators.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the solutions from a multi-objective vehicle routing problem (VRP) that minimizes both cost and emissions, we can identify the precise policy parameter values that would align operator incentives with societal environmental goals, thereby closing the loop between operational optimization and policy design.
proposals.method: We will first model a time-dependent, selective VRP with stochastic demand, minimizing total cost and CO2 emissions using a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (MOEA), such as NSGA-II or MOEA/D. This generates a Pareto front of efficient routing solutions. Subsequently, we develop an inverse optimization module that, for each Pareto-optimal solution, solves for the policy parameters (e.g., tax per ton of CO2, subsidy per low-emission vehicle) that make that solution also optimal for a profit-maximizing operator. This creates an iterative feedback loop between the evolutionary search and policy parameter space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for evidence-based environmental policy design in transportation logistics. It will advance the field of inverse optimization by applying it to complex, multi-objective combinatorial problems under uncertainty, offering policymakers a tool to anticipate the operational impacts of regulatory measures before implementation.
proposals.novelty: The key innovation is the integration of evolutionary multi-objective optimization for VRPs with an inverse design loop for policy parameters, moving beyond simulating policy impacts to actively designing policies that induce desired operational behaviors. This direct coupling of solution and policy spaces is a novel application in sustainable logistics.

proposals.title: A Learning-Based Branching Strategy for the Weighted Maximum Independent Set Problem in Fleet and Service Network Design
proposals.motivation: The Weighted Maximum Independent Set (WMIS) problem is a fundamental NP-hard combinatorial optimization challenge with direct applications in resource allocation, scheduling, and network design, such as selecting non-conflicting service routes or fleet deployment plans to maximize profit or service level. Pre-2026, exact solution methods like branch-and-bound rely heavily on hand-crafted branching rules, which can be inefficient for large-scale, real-world instances common in logistics and postal operations. Developing more intelligent, adaptive branching strategies is crucial for solving these practical problems to optimality within reasonable time limits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a graph neural network (GNN) can be trained to predict high-quality branching variables for WMIS, leveraging the problem's graph structure and vertex weights, and that integrating this learned policy into a branch-and-bound solver will significantly reduce solution times compared to classical branching rules on logistics-inspired instances.
proposals.method: We will extend the learning-to-branch framework, successful for the unweighted MIS, to the weighted case. Our approach involves: 1) Generating a diverse dataset of WMIS instances derived from fleet deployment and service network models, incorporating time-dependent demands and costs. 2) Training a GNN architecture (e.g., using message-passing networks) on state representations of the branch-and-bound tree to predict the vertex that, when branched on, leads to the largest reduction in the dual bound, using imitation learning from strong branching. 3) Implementing the trained model within an exact MILP solver (e.g., SCIP) to guide the search, with a fallback to classical rules for robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated learning-based branching strategy for the Weighted Maximum Independent Set problem, directly applicable to fleet composition and service selection models under uncertainty. It will advance the integration of machine learning into exact optimization for logistics, offering a new, scalable tool for operations researchers. The resulting solver enhancements will be empirically validated on benchmark and real-world inspired instances.
proposals.novelty: While learning-to-branch has been explored for generic MILPs, its tailored application to WMIS, especially for weighted graphs modeling profit/cost in logistics contexts, is novel. The key innovation is adapting the representation and training objective to simultaneously capture graph topology and vertex weight information, moving beyond unweighted graph properties.

proposals.title: A Branch-and-Price Algorithm for the Vehicle Routing Problem with Pickup and Delivery and Time Windows (VRPPDTW)
proposals.motivation: The Vehicle Routing Problem with Pickup and Delivery (VRPPD) is central to modern logistics, from courier services to less-than-truckload shipping. In practice, customer availability imposes strict time windows on both pickup and delivery operations, a critical constraint not fully integrated into core VRPPD models. Addressing the VRPPDTW is essential for generating operationally feasible and cost-effective plans that respect real-world service commitments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a branch-and-price exact algorithm, leveraging strong set-partitioning formulations and tailored labeling algorithms for the time-window constrained pickup and delivery elementary shortest path subproblem, can solve realistic instances of the VRPPDTW to optimality, providing new benchmarks and insights into problem structure.
proposals.method: The core methodology will be an exact branch-and-price algorithm. The master problem will be a set-partitioning formulation. The pricing subproblem will be an Elementary Shortest Path Problem with Resource Constraints (ESPPRC) for pickup and delivery, extended with time windows. We will develop a dynamic programming labeling algorithm with dominance rules and bidirectional search to solve this subproblem efficiently. Acceleration techniques, including heuristic column generation and strong branching rules, will be integrated. Computational experiments will be conducted on adapted Solomon instances and real-world inspired data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated, exact branch-and-price framework for the VRPPDTW, establishing new optimality benchmarks. It will advance the methodological toolkit for rich vehicle routing by formally integrating time windows into the combinatorial structure of paired demands. The analysis will reveal the computational impact of time windows on pickup and delivery problems.
proposals.novelty: While branch-and-price is known for VRPTW and heuristic methods exist for VRPPD, the novel integration for the exact solution of VRPPDTW is non-trivial, requiring fundamental extensions to the ESPPRC labeling algorithm to handle both paired demand precedence and dual time-window resources simultaneously.

proposals.title: A Min-Max and Bi-Objective Framework for the Vehicle Routing Problem with Pickup and Delivery: Balancing Equity and Efficiency
proposals.motivation: In logistics and postal operations, the classic VRPPD minimizes total cost, often leading to highly unequal workloads among drivers, which impacts service quality and driver satisfaction. Pre-2026, there is growing operational research interest in equitable resource allocation and robust planning under uncertainty, yet the VRPPD is seldom studied under min-max (minimizing the longest route) or explicit multi-objective (balancing route length and fleet size) frameworks critical for real-world fleet management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reformulating the VRPPD with a min-max objective or a bi-objective function combining total distance and fleet size will yield solution sets that significantly improve route equity and operational flexibility without disproportionate sacrifices in overall efficiency, compared to traditional cost-minimization models.
proposals.method: We will develop mathematical formulations for the min-max VRPPD and a bi-objective VRPPD (minimizing total distance and number of vehicles). Solution methods will build upon Laporte's expertise in exact and metaheuristic approaches for routing and scheduling. Specifically, we will adapt adaptive large neighborhood search (ALNS) and branch-and-cut algorithms, incorporating techniques from recent work on the selective TSP with time-dependent profits to handle the added complexity of the equity objective. The bi-objective model will be solved using an epsilon-constraint method to generate Pareto-optimal frontiers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide new models and solution algorithms for the VRPPD that directly address driver workload balancing and strategic fleet sizing. It will contribute to the literature on equitable routing and multi-objective optimization in transportation, offering decision-makers a set of trade-off solutions for more sustainable and fair operational plans.
proposals.novelty: While min-max objectives exist for basic VRP, their application to the paired pickup and delivery constraints is novel. Furthermore, explicitly modeling the trade-off between total distance and fleet size within the VRPPD framework addresses a gap in strategic-operational planning integration not covered by standard cost-focused models.

proposals.title: Inverse Fleet Routing: Inferring Cost Structures from Observed Ship Routes under Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: In maritime logistics and postal operations, observed routes (e.g., from AIS data) reflect complex trade-offs between distance, time, fuel, and service constraints. However, the underlying cost structure that drives these routing decisions is often proprietary or unobservable. Inferring this structure from data is crucial for competitors, regulators, and planners to understand market dynamics, benchmark performance, and predict fleet behavior under new policies, especially in uncertain environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the implicit cost and distance metrics governing a fleet's routing decisions can be accurately reconstructed by solving an inverse optimization problem, where the observed routes are assumed to be optimal or near-optimal solutions to a stochastic or time-dependent vehicle routing problem.
proposals.method: We will formulate this as an inverse optimization model. Given a set of observed routes, we will assume they are solutions to a fleet deployment problem with a liner service structure, possibly under uncertainty as in Laporte's prior work. The core model will be a mathematical program that adjusts edge costs in a graph (representing distance, time, or composite cost) to minimize the gap between the observed routes and the optimal routes of a forward problem (e.g., a selective traveling salesman or vehicle routing problem). We will employ column generation and cutting-plane methods to handle large-scale instances, and test the approach using historical shipping and postal delivery data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for inverse analysis in fleet routing, extending inverse optimization from simple shortest path problems to complex operational research contexts with service constraints and uncertainty. It will deliver practical tools for logistics analysts to reverse-engineer competitor cost models and validate the consistency of observed operations with stated cost objectives.
proposals.novelty: While inverse shortest path problems are studied, applying inverse optimization to infer costs from solutions to rich fleet routing problems with time-dependent profits, selective visitation, and stochastic elements—core themes in Laporte's recent work—is a novel and non-trivial extension.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Cooperative Game Framework for Multi-Depot Vehicle Routing with Mobile Resources
proposals.motivation: The rise of urban consolidation centers, mobile parcel lockers, and electric vehicle charging infrastructure introduces a new paradigm where key logistics resources are not fixed but mobile. This creates a complex, time-dependent network topology for multi-depot routing. For competing logistics firms, collaboration via resource sharing can reduce costs and improve service, but the dynamic nature of mobile assets makes traditional, static coalition formation and cost allocation models unstable and unfair over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating concepts from dynamic cooperative games with stochastic multi-depot vehicle routing models will yield stable, long-term coalitions and fair cost allocations, even when the marginal contribution of a firm's mobile resources fluctuates due to spatio-temporal demand shifts.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage stochastic integer programming model. The first stage decides the coalition structure and the positioning of shared mobile resources (e.g., depots, charging points). The second stage solves a fleet operation problem under demand uncertainty, building on Laporte's work on ship fleet uncertainty. The core of the cost allocation will be based on the dynamic Shapley value, approximated via a sampling-based algorithm to handle computational complexity. Stability will be tested using the dynamic core concept.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, integrated methodology for strategic coalition formation and tactical resource positioning in dynamic logistics networks. It will extend cooperative game theory into dynamic, stochastic routing contexts and offer practitioners a framework for designing equitable, long-term collaboration contracts for shared mobile infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of dynamic cooperative game theory with multi-depot vehicle routing where depots themselves are mobile and decisions are two-fold (coalition formation *and* resource positioning) is a novel integration beyond static resource sharing or fixed-network collaboration models.

proposals.title: A Matheuristic Framework for the Fleet Deployment and Routing Problem with Stochastic Demand
proposals.motivation: In maritime logistics and postal operations, fleet deployment and vehicle routing decisions are interdependent and must be made under demand uncertainty to balance cost and service levels. Pre-2026 research often treats these problems sequentially or simplifies uncertainty, leading to suboptimal, fragile plans. A unified approach is needed to provide robust, cost-effective solutions for operators managing heterogeneous fleets over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tightly integrated matheuristic, combining exact methods for strategic fleet allocation with adaptive metaheuristics for operational routing under uncertainty, will yield significantly better solutions than sequential or purely heuristic approaches for this class of problems.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer framework. The upper layer uses integer programming (e.g., branch-and-price) to solve a deterministic fleet allocation and liner service design problem. The lower layer employs adaptive large neighborhood search (ALNS) metaheuristics to solve time-dependent, stochastic VRPs for each assigned vessel, generating dual bounds and feasibility cuts. These layers will communicate iteratively: the ALNS provides detailed routing costs and stochastic service metrics to refine the master problem, while the master problem's solution guides the search space of the metaheuristics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable hybrid solution architecture for complex logistics planning under uncertainty. It will advance the methodology of matheuristics by demonstrating a principled, bidirectional information exchange between exact and heuristic components. Practically, it will deliver a decision-support tool for integrated fleet and route planning with quantifiable service guarantees.
proposals.novelty: While matheuristics exist, our framework's novelty lies in the explicit, iterative coupling of exact strategic planning with metaheuristic operational planning under stochastic demand, moving beyond using one method merely to initialize or post-optimize the other.

proposals.title: A Shapley Value Framework for Fair and Stable Cost Allocation in Collaborative Vehicle Routing
proposals.motivation: Collaborative logistics, where multiple partners share vehicle fleets, offers significant cost-saving potential but is hindered by the critical challenge of fairly allocating the realized savings. Traditional proportional methods can be unfair and lead to coalitional instability, preventing long-term cooperation. A principled, incentive-compatible allocation method is needed to foster sustainable horizontal collaboration in supply chains.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the Shapley Value from cooperative game theory and its recent applications in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) for reward attribution will yield a cost allocation scheme for collaborative VRPs that is perceived as fairer, promotes coalition stability, and provides stronger incentives for participation than standard proportional rules.
proposals.method: We will formulate the collaborative VRP with shared depot and heterogeneous demands as a cooperative game. The core computational challenge is efficiently calculating the Shapley Value, which requires solving the underlying VRP for all possible coalitions of partners. We will develop a tailored algorithm combining: 1) a column generation heuristic for the coalitional VRP subproblems, and 2) approximation techniques for the Shapley Value (e.g., based on random sampling of coalition permutations) to manage computational complexity. The framework will be tested on benchmark instances and realistic data from postal and freight sectors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, game-theoretic computational tool for cost allocation in collaborative transportation, directly addressing a key barrier to implementation. It will extend the analytical toolkit for collaborative VRPs beyond mere cost calculation to include stability and fairness analysis. The research will also demonstrate a valuable cross-pollination of concepts from distributed AI to operations research.
proposals.novelty: While the Shapley Value is known in game theory, its systematic adaptation and computational implementation for cost allocation in collaborative VRPs, inspired by its use for fairness in MARL systems, is novel. This approach moves beyond simple proportional splits to a marginal contribution-based scheme grounded in coalitional rationality.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Learning-Exact Pricing Framework for Scalable Branch-and-Price in Rich Vehicle Routing Problems
proposals.motivation: Branch-and-price is a powerful method for complex Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs) with rich constraints, such as time windows, heterogeneous fleets, and selective demands, which are central to real-world postal and logistics operations. However, its scalability is severely limited by the computational bottleneck of solving the NP-hard pricing subproblem exactly at every iteration. This hinders its application to industrial-scale problems where operational decisions must balance cost and service quality under uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a machine learning model as a fast heuristic pricing oracle to propose promising columns, with a fallback to exact dynamic programming, will significantly accelerate column generation convergence while preserving optimality guarantees, making large-scale rich VRP optimization tractable.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid pricing system where a Graph Neural Network (GNN), trained offline on historical pricing subproblem solutions, predicts columns with high negative reduced cost. This heuristic proposal will be integrated into a column generation loop; only if it fails to find a negative reduced cost column will the exact labeling algorithm (e.g., for the Elementary Shortest Path Problem with Resource Constraints) be invoked.
