The framework will be embedded within a standard branch-and-price solver and tested on rich VRP variants, including the Time-Dependent Profitable Tour Problem and fleet deployment problems under demand uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, accelerated optimization framework for complex VRPs that retains the convergence and optimality guarantees of exact methods. It will advance the methodological toolkit for large-scale logistics planning, directly contributing to more efficient resource allocation and fleet management under uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied as a standalone heuristic for VRPs, its tight integration as an *oracle* within an exact branch-and-price algorithm to overcome the core pricing bottleneck is a novel synthesis, creating a robust hybrid that leverages the speed of learning and the guarantee of traditional methods.

proposals.title: A Constraint-Based Optimization Model for Integrated Multi-Echelon Supply Chain Routing
proposals.motivation: Global supply chains are complex, multi-echelon networks where routing decisions at one level (e.g., raw material transport) directly impact costs and service at others (e.g., last-mile delivery). Traditional sequential optimization leads to suboptimal system-wide performance, especially under demand uncertainty and capacity constraints. A holistic, computable framework is needed to optimize material and financial flows from source to consumer simultaneously.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a multi-echelon supply chain as a directed metabolic network and applying Constraint-Based Reconstruction and Analysis (COBRA) principles, we can formulate a tractable linear programming model that yields globally optimal routing and allocation decisions, improving total system profit and service level compared to sequential or decentralized approaches.
proposals.method: We will construct a directed network where nodes represent facilities (suppliers, plants, DCs, retailers) and arcs represent transport links. Inspired by COBRA, we will formulate a large-scale Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) model. The objective is to maximize profit (revenue minus costs for transport, inventory, tariffs) or service level. Constraints will mirror metabolic constraints: flow conservation (mass balance), arc capacities (enzyme limits), and external demand (growth objective). We will solve the model using commercial solvers (e.g., CPLEX, Gurobi) and develop decomposition heuristics (e.g., Dantzig-Wolfe) for large-scale instances, incorporating stochastic demand scenarios based on Laporte's prior work on fleet uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, unified optimization framework for integrated supply chain routing, bridging operations research and systems biology. It will deliver a benchmark for holistic performance and generate insights into the value of centralized coordination. The model will also serve as a testbed for analyzing trade-offs between cost, service, and sustainability (e.g., carbon flux limits).
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct methodological analogy from systems biology (COBRA) to supply chain optimization, treating the entire chain as a single metabolic system. This provides a fundamentally different perspective from traditional sequential or hierarchical OR models for multi-echelon routing and allocation problems.

proposals.title: A Robust Optimization Framework for Multi-Echelon Maritime and Land Routing with Endogenous Uncertainty Propagation
proposals.motivation: Global supply chains face cascading disruptions from correlated demand shocks and port delays, as seen during the COVID-19 pandemic and Suez Canal blockage. Traditional robust optimization treats uncertainty as exogenous, failing to capture how tactical routing decisions at one echelon (e.g., liner shipping) reshape the risk profile for subsequent echelons (e.g., regional trucking). This leads to fragile, sub-optimal plans that compromise cost and service.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the endogenous propagation of uncertainty between echelons—where first-stage routing decisions define the feasible uncertainty sets for second-stage fulfillment—will yield a coordinated defensive strategy that is both more resilient and cost-effective than standard robust or stochastic approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage adaptive robust optimization model integrating multi-echelon routing. The first stage decides primary vessel and major lane assignments, while the second stage adapts regional truck and feeder ship routes to realized demand and delays. Crucially, the first-stage decisions will constrain the polyhedral uncertainty set (inspired by finance) for the second stage, modeling correlation and propagation. We will solve it using a column-and-constraint generation (C&CG) algorithm, enhanced by decomposition heuristics from fleet management literature to ensure tractability for realistic instances.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first robust optimization framework for integrated global routing that explicitly models endogenous uncertainty propagation. It will deliver a computationally tractable decision-support tool for designing resilient multi-echelon routes, contributing new methodological insights at the intersection of robust optimization and maritime logistics.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the endogenous modeling of uncertainty propagation across echelons within a robust framework, a significant departure from existing models that treat uncertainties as independent or exogenous at each stage.

proposals.title: Inverse Network Design for Logistics: Determining Depot Locations from Target Routing Costs
proposals.motivation: Strategic infrastructure planning, such as locating postal depots or fleet hubs, is traditionally driven by cost minimization for a given demand. However, public and private logistics operators often face budget constraints or service-level agreements that define acceptable operational cost targets. The inverse Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) framework, which determines input parameters (like depot locations) needed to achieve a desired output (like a routing cost), is a novel and practically relevant approach to this strategic decision problem, aligning infrastructure investment with operational performance goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given set of customer demands and a target total routing cost, it is possible to formulate and solve an inverse optimization model to identify a set of feasible depot locations, and that this solution set can inform robust network design decisions under uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will develop a bi-level mathematical programming formulation. The upper level selects depot locations from candidate sites to minimize deviation from a target fleet routing cost. The lower level is a classical multi-depot VRP evaluating the operational cost for a given configuration. Solution approaches will leverage Laporte's expertise in exact methods (e.g., branch-and-cut for the selective TSP) and metaheuristics. We will extend the model to incorporate uncertainty in demand and travel times, drawing on methods from 'How to operate ship fleets under uncertainty', using stochastic programming or robust optimization techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new methodological framework for the inverse VRP applied to network design, providing planners with a tool to back-calculate infrastructure needs from performance targets. It will also yield computational insights into solving bi-level models combining location and routing, and extend the application of inverse optimization in logistics beyond simple parameter calibration.
proposals.novelty: While inverse optimization has been studied for calibrating model parameters, its application to determine physical depot locations given a desired routing cost target is novel. Furthermore, integrating this inverse perspective with established stochastic VRP models from Laporte's recent work creates a unique bridge between strategic infrastructure design and robust operational planning.

proposals.title: A Robust Fleet Allocation Model for Maximizing Serviced Demand with Quality-of-Service Guarantees
proposals.motivation: In logistics and postal operations, operators face the dual challenge of maximizing throughput and maintaining service quality to retain customers, especially under uncertain demand. Pre-2026 literature often treats these objectives separately, focusing either on cost minimization or service level optimization, which can lead to plans that are either inefficient or unreliable in practice. A unified framework is needed to balance these competing goals explicitly for a fixed fleet.
proposals.hypothesis: A robust optimization model that integrates explicit, enforceable constraints on wait times, time window adherence, and workload equity can generate Pareto-efficient operational plans that significantly improve the practical balance between serviced demand volume and service reliability compared to traditional sequential or cost-focused approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage robust optimization model with recourse, where first-stage decisions assign vehicles to depots/areas, and second-stage recourse actions handle demand realization. Service quality will be encoded as hard constraints or penalty terms in the objective for wait times and time window violations. Workload equity will be modeled using constraints on vehicle utilization (e.g., min-max tour duration). Solution methods will leverage matheuristics, combining mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) solvers for the core model with adaptive large neighborhood search (ALNS) to handle larger instances and the combinatorial complexity of routing.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel decision-support tool for fleet managers in postal and logistics sectors, offering a set of viable trade-off solutions. It will advance the integration of robust optimization with multi-objective vehicle routing, contributing new models and solution algorithms to the operational research literature on fleet management under uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The explicit joint optimization of serviced demand volume and enforceable service quality metrics (wait time, time windows, workload balance) within a single robust framework represents a significant departure from models that treat service as a secondary objective or a soft constraint without guaranteed bounds.

proposals.title: A Fleet-Constrained Service Maximization Model for Time-Dependent Demand in Urban Logistics
proposals.motivation: In urban logistics and postal operations, fleet size is often fixed in the short term due to capital constraints. The classic operational research problem minimizes cost for a given demand, but a critical dual problem—maximizing serviced demand or customer visits with a fixed fleet—is understudied. This is vital for service providers facing volatile, time-dependent demand, such as same-day delivery or dynamic service routing, where the goal is optimal resource utilization to enhance service level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating the problem as a selective routing and scheduling model with time-dependent profits, we can develop efficient exact and heuristic algorithms that identify service-maximizing configurations significantly outperforming standard cost-minimization approaches under the same fleet constraints.
proposals.method: We will model the problem as a Generalized Selective Traveling Salesman Problem with Time Windows and Time-Dependent Profits, where each customer has a profit (demand weight) and a time-dependent serviceability function. The core methodology involves a branch-and-price exact algorithm for smaller instances, leveraging column generation for route enumeration. For larger, realistic instances, we will develop an Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search (ALNS) metaheuristic, incorporating destroy/repair operators tailored to prioritize high-profit, temporally feasible customers, building on pre-2026 success in vehicle routing and fleet optimization under uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, service-oriented optimization framework for fixed-fleet operations, complementing traditional cost-focused models. It will deliver benchmark solutions and scalable algorithms for logistics managers to quantify the service capacity of their existing assets. The research will extend the theoretical understanding of selective routing problems by integrating hard fleet constraints with time-dependent profit maximization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in rigorously treating the 'dual' operational problem of service maximization under a fixed fleet, explicitly integrating time-dependent demand and serviceability, which is distinct from the prevalent cost-minimization or profit-maximization models with fleet size decisions.

proposals.title: A Fleet Utilization Model for Maximizing Trips Under a Fixed Operational Budget
proposals.motivation: In logistics and service network design (e.g., postal delivery, liner shipping), the classical objective is to minimize cost or time for a given set of demands. However, under capital-intensive constraints, a critical managerial question is how to maximize the productive output of a fixed fleet given a strict operational budget. This inverted perspective directly addresses asset utilization, a key performance metric in pre-2026 vehicle routing and fleet management literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by inverting the classical objective to maximize the number of completed trips (or serviced demand points) subject to a hard constraint on total operational cost or time, we can identify routing and allocation strategies that significantly improve fleet utilization without exceeding budget, leading to more efficient resource deployment.
proposals.method: We will formulate this as a novel variant of the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) with a hard budget constraint and an objective to maximize served trips. The method builds upon Prof. Laporte's expertise in stochastic and selective routing (e.g., Selective TSP). We will develop a metaheuristic framework, likely a hybrid Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search (ALNS) combined with exact methods for sub-problems, to handle medium to large instances. The model will incorporate uncertainty in travel times and demand, extending methods from 'How to operate ship fleets under uncertainty', to ensure robust utilization plans.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new optimization model and solution algorithms for high-utilization routing under budget caps. It will provide decision-support tools for fleet managers in postal and liner shipping sectors to extract maximum service from limited assets. The research will also expand the theoretical understanding of budget-constrained, profit-maximizing routing problems.
proposals.novelty: While VRPs typically minimize cost for full service, our model inverts this to maximize service for a fixed cost, a non-trivial shift in perspective with distinct combinatorial structure. Integrating this inverted objective with stochastic elements for robust utilization planning is not extensively covered in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Two-Stage Stochastic Programming Model for Fleet Management with Stochastic Travel Times and Demands
proposals.motivation: In real-world logistics, such as postal and maritime operations, planners face significant uncertainty in travel times (due to congestion, weather) and customer demands. Deterministic models, while foundational, can lead to inefficient fleet utilization, missed service time windows, and excessive costs when reality deviates from forecasts. Addressing these dual uncertainties is critical for robust operational planning and cost-effective service delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-stage stochastic programming model, incorporating correlated stochastic travel times and demand quantities, will yield fleet management and routing solutions that are more robust and cost-effective than those derived from deterministic or single-source uncertainty models.
proposals.method: We will formulate a two-stage stochastic integer program. The first stage decides fleet composition and liner service schedules. The second stage, for a set of discrete scenarios, determines vehicle routes and customer service decisions, penalizing recourse actions like demand outsourcing or overtime. We will solve it using a Sample Average Approximation (SAA) framework combined with a branch-and-cut algorithm. For larger instances, we will develop a metaheuristic (e.g., Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search) that integrates scenario-based evaluation within the search process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified modeling and algorithmic framework for fleet management under dual operational uncertainties. It will extend the literature on stochastic vehicle routing by explicitly linking strategic fleet decisions with tactical routing recourse. The computational study will offer managerial insights into the cost of uncertainty and the value of stochastic solutions for logistics operators.
proposals.novelty: While stochastic travel times and demands have been studied separately, their joint treatment in a combined fleet composition and routing model is novel. The integration of SAA with specialized algorithms for this integrated problem extends beyond the typical focus on routing alone, aligning with Laporte's recent work on fleet uncertainty and selective TSP variants.

proposals.title: A Multi-Trip Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows: Modeling and Solution Methods for Urban Logistics
proposals.motivation: In urban logistics and postal delivery, vehicles often perform multiple trips per shift to meet high demand, while customers increasingly require specific delivery time windows. The Multi-Trip VRP (MTVRP) efficiently allocates trips to vehicles but largely ignores time window constraints, while the VRP with Time Windows (VRPTW) assumes single trips. Integrating these constraints is crucial for realistic, cost-effective planning that balances fleet utilization and service quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating time windows into the MTVRP will yield significantly different and more operationally realistic routing plans compared to sequential or decoupled planning, and that this integrated problem can be solved effectively using tailored metaheuristic algorithms.
proposals.method: We will formulate a Mixed-Integer Programming model for the MTVRP-TW. Given the problem's complexity, we will develop a hybrid adaptive large neighborhood search (ALNS) heuristic, enhanced with tailored destroy/repair operators for trip sequencing and time window feasibility. This will be integrated with a set partitioning-based matheuristic for post-optimization, drawing on Laporte's expertise in exact and heuristic methods for routing and scheduling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated model and solution framework for the MTVRP-TW, closing a gap between two well-studied VRP variants. It will offer logistics managers a practical tool for simultaneous fleet sizing, trip scheduling, and time-sensitive routing, directly applicable to postal, parcel, and same-day delivery services.
proposals.novelty: While MTVRP and VRPTW are mature fields, their combination is novel. The core challenge lies in synchronizing the trip sequencing and vehicle assignment decisions of the MTVRP with the strict temporal feasibility of the VRPTW, a non-trivial integration not addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Column Generation Approach for the Multi-Trip Vehicle Routing Problem with Time-Dependent Demand
proposals.motivation: In postal and express delivery operations, vehicles often perform multiple trips per day to meet dynamic, time-dependent customer demand while adhering to strict service time windows. Traditional Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) models often treat vehicles as single-trip resources, failing to capture the complex scheduling and fleet allocation challenges of multi-trip operations. This gap leads to suboptimal fleet utilization and higher operational costs for logistics providers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting the column generation framework, a cornerstone of large-scale airline crew scheduling, to the multi-trip VRP, we can develop a computationally efficient method that yields significantly lower-cost schedules than existing monolithic VRP formulations.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a set partitioning master problem over feasible vehicle 'duties' (sequences of trips with breaks). The pricing subproblem, generating negative reduced cost duties, will be modeled as a resource-constrained shortest path problem on a time-space network, incorporating time-dependent travel times and profits (demand fulfillment). This subproblem will be solved using dynamic programming with dominance rules, akin to methods in crew pairing. The overall algorithm will be embedded in a branch-and-price framework to obtain integer solutions.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, scalable optimization model for multi-trip VRPs with explicit time-dependent considerations. It will provide practitioners with a powerful planning tool to reduce fleet size and operational costs in dynamic logistics. The work will also extend the application domain of column generation, demonstrating its transferability from crew scheduling to complex vehicle scheduling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct and non-trivial translation of the crew pairing column generation architecture—typically concerned with labor rules and crew bases—to the domain of multi-trip vehicle scheduling with physical asset constraints and time-dependent urban traffic patterns, a synthesis not deeply explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Constraint Programming Framework for Fleet Management with Complex Time-Dependent and Capacity Side Constraints
proposals.motivation: In maritime logistics and postal delivery, operational planning must adhere to intricate side constraints, such as time-dependent port access windows, crew rest rules, and heterogeneous vehicle capacity limits. Traditional linear and mixed-integer programming models, while strong for core routing and allocation, often struggle to efficiently encode and propagate these complex, non-linear rules, leading to suboptimal or infeasible solutions in practice. This gap is evident in pre-2026 work on fleet operations under uncertainty and time-dependent profits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dedicated Constraint Programming (CP) model, leveraging global constraints and domain filtering, can more effectively and declaratively handle these side constraints than standard MIP formulations, leading to improved solution feasibility and quality for real-world fleet management problems.
proposals.method: We will develop a CP model using a state-of-the-art solver (e.g., IBM ILOG CP Optimizer). The model will define decision variables for vehicle assignments, sequences, and start times. Key global constraints (e.g., `noOverlap`, `cumulative`, `sequence`) will model time windows, travel times, and capacity. Complex rules, such as time-dependent service profits and mandatory rest periods, will be encoded via custom constraints and expressions. This CP model will be integrated within a hybrid CP-MIP search framework or used as a feasibility/neighborhood search component within a larger metaheuristic for the selective TSP and fleet deployment problems.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, reusable CP modeling template for complex side constraints in vehicle routing and fleet management. It will deliver computational benchmarks demonstrating CP's advantages in feasibility checking and solution quality for problems with intricate temporal and regulatory rules. The work will extend the methodological toolkit in operational research, offering practitioners a more robust approach for real-world planning.
proposals.novelty: While CP is established in scheduling, its tailored application to the specific, complex side constraints of maritime and postal fleet management—integrating time-dependent profits and uncertainty considerations from Laporte's recent work—represents a novel synthesis. The proposed hybrid CP-MIP architecture for these domains is non-obvious compared to pure optimization or simulation methods prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Large-Scale Stochastic Fleet Allocation Model for City-Wide Same-Day Delivery in E-Commerce
proposals.motivation: The rapid growth of e-commerce has intensified demand for same-day delivery (SDD), creating a critical operational challenge for logistics providers. Operating a fleet to meet stochastic, spatially dispersed customer demands across an entire city requires sophisticated planning to balance service promises against prohibitive costs. Existing models often lack the scale or the integrated treatment of uncertainty and time-dependent routing necessary for real-world urban SDD networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-stage stochastic programming model, combined with advanced metaheuristics and cluster-first-route-second decomposition, can produce robust, cost-effective daily operational plans for city-wide SDD fleets under demand uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will formulate a two-stage stochastic program where first-stage decisions allocate vehicles to depots/zones, and second-stage recourse actions solve time-constrained routing under sampled demand scenarios. The model builds on Laporte's work on fleet operations under uncertainty and selective TSPs. To solve large instances, we will develop a hybrid solution architecture: a large neighborhood search (LNS) metaheuristic will handle the fleet allocation, while adaptive large neighborhood search (ALNS) solvers, warmed with initial routes from a time-dependent savings algorithm, will manage the embedded vehicle routing problems with time windows (VRPTW).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, integrated decision-support tool for SDD fleet management that explicitly trades off cost, service level, and robustness. It will advance the methodological literature on stochastic fleet management by demonstrating a practical decomposition for city-scale problems and offer actionable insights for logistics planners in the e-commerce sector.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tailored integration of a stochastic allocation model with high-performance, time-dependent routing heuristics at a metropolitan scale, specifically designed for the dynamic, high-volume context of e-commerce SDD, which remains under-explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Fleet Minimization Model for Multi-Trip Vehicle Routing with Guaranteed Service Levels
proposals.motivation: In logistics and postal operations, fleet sizing is a critical capital expenditure decision. Traditional models often minimize cost for a fixed fleet or maximize service for a given fleet. The dual problem—finding the absolute minimum fleet size required to serve all demand with a guaranteed service level (e.g., all customers served within a time window)—is less studied but crucial for strategic planning and green logistics, as it directly targets resource efficiency. This is particularly relevant for operations where vehicles can perform multiple trips per day, such as in urban delivery or liner shipping feeder services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the fleet size as the primary objective within a multi-trip vehicle routing problem (VRP) framework, we can develop exact and heuristic methods that yield significantly smaller, yet operationally feasible, fleet configurations compared to those derived from cost-minimization models, without compromising service commitments.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a Mixed-Integer Program (MIP) with the objective of minimizing the number of vehicles. Constraints will include time windows, vehicle capacity, maximum route duration, and the possibility of multiple trips per vehicle. Given the problem's complexity, we will develop a branch-and-price exact algorithm, leveraging column generation where columns represent feasible routes and trips. For larger instances, a metaheuristic framework (e.g., an Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search) will be designed, incorporating specialized destroy/repair operators that aggressively consolidate trips onto fewer vehicles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new optimization model and solution methods for a fundamental strategic logistics problem. It will contribute to the VRP literature by offering a rigorous dual perspective to fleet management. Practically, it will deliver decision-support tools for logistics managers to determine the most resource-efficient fleet for a given service contract.
proposals.novelty: While multi-trip VRPs exist, the explicit minimization of fleet size as the sole objective, decoupled from variable routing costs, represents a novel and non-trivial combinatorial challenge. The integration of guaranteed service-level constraints into this pure fleet-minimization context distinguishes it from prior cost-based or profit-oriented models.

proposals.title: Inverse Fleet Sizing via Minimum Workday Duration for Fixed Vehicle Routes
proposals.motivation: In postal and logistics operations, fixed delivery routes are often designed first, but their feasibility depends on vehicle availability and driver workday regulations. A critical, underexplored problem is determining the minimum fleet workday length required to operate a given set of routes, which directly impacts labor costs and service-level agreements. This inverse perspective complements traditional fleet sizing and vehicle routing problems by providing a lower bound for operational planning under fixed route constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given set of fixed routes with stochastic service and travel times, the minimum required workday duration can be formulated as a stochastic optimization problem and solved efficiently, providing a robust basis for fleet management decisions.
proposals.method: We will model the problem as a stochastic program where the objective is to minimize the maximum route duration across the fleet, subject to time-window and demand satisfaction constraints inherent in the fixed routes. The core will be a two-stage approach: first, a deterministic analysis using linear programming to bound durations based on average speeds and service times; second, a simulation-based optimization incorporating time-dependent travel times and demand uncertainty, solved via a metaheuristic (e.g., a tailored tabu search) to find robust workday minima. The method will be validated on benchmark instances from postal logistics and liner shipping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for inverse operational planning, allowing managers to assess the feasibility of proposed routes against workday limits. It will contribute a new optimization model to the fleet management literature, bridging deterministic routing and stochastic scheduling. The resulting tools can directly inform cost and service trade-offs in postal and freight distribution networks.
proposals.novelty: While most literature minimizes fleet size or cost for a given workday, we invert the problem to find the minimum workday for fixed routes, a perspective rarely studied. Integrating this with realistic time-dependent and stochastic elements, aligned with Laporte's recent work on uncertainty and selective TSP, provides a distinct combinatorial challenge.

proposals.title: A Multi-Period, Multi-Trip Inventory Routing Model with Stochastic Demand for Fleet and Service Cost Optimization
proposals.motivation: Integrated inventory routing problems (IRP) are critical for logistics efficiency, yet most models treat vehicle routes as single trips within a single day, decoupling them from multi-day inventory dynamics. Real-world operations, such as postal delivery, retail replenishment, or maritime container shipping, involve fleets making multiple trips per vehicle per day while managing customer inventories over a planning horizon. This gap leads to suboptimal fleet utilization and inflated costs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the joint optimization of multi-trip vehicle routing and multi-period inventory policies under demand uncertainty will yield significantly lower total system costs—encompassing routing, inventory holding, and shortage penalties—compared to sequential or single-period approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage stochastic programming model. The first stage decides multi-period inventory policies and fleet allocation, while the second stage, for each demand scenario, solves a time-dependent, multi-trip vehicle routing problem. To solve this complex integration, we will design a matheuristic combining a branch-and-cut framework for the strategic inventory and fleet decisions with adaptive large neighborhood search (ALNS) for the operational routing. The liner shipping and fleet management methodologies from Laporte's prior work will inform the cost and service constraint structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified modeling and algorithmic framework for a practically significant but computationally challenging class of IRPs. It will deliver managerial insights into the trade-offs between fleet size, vehicle utilization, and safety stock levels over time. The solution methods will advance the state-of-the-art in handling integrated logistics planning under uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty lies in the simultaneous consideration of three complex dimensions typically studied in isolation: multi-trip routing (allowing multiple routes per vehicle per day), explicit multi-period inventory coordination, and stochastic demand, all within a single optimization model.

proposals.title: A Multi-Objective Optimization Model for the Pollution-Routing Problem with a Heterogeneous Vehicle Fleet
proposals.motivation: The Pollution-Routing Problem (PRP) is critical for reducing the environmental impact of logistics. Prior PRP models often assume a homogeneous fleet, which is unrealistic for many carriers who operate vehicles with varying fuel consumption, emission rates, and capacities. Addressing this heterogeneity is essential for generating practical, cost-effective routing plans that accurately balance operational expenditure and environmental externalities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling a mixed fleet of conventional, hybrid, and electric vehicles within a time-dependent PRP framework will yield routing and fleet allocation solutions that significantly reduce total cost (fuel, emissions, and fixed vehicle costs) compared to models assuming fleet homogeneity or single objectives.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-objective mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model that integrates vehicle-specific emission and fuel cost functions with time-dependent travel and service times. To solve realistic instances, we will design a metaheuristic framework, likely a hybrid adaptive large neighborhood search (ALNS) combined with a tailored branch-and-cut procedure for fleet assignment. The method will be validated using standard PRP benchmark instances adapted with heterogeneous vehicle data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive modeling and solution approach for the PRP with heterogeneous fleets, directly extending the work of Bektas and Laporte (2011). It will offer decision-makers a tool to analyze trade-offs between cost and emissions under realistic fleet constraints, contributing to both the green logistics and vehicle routing literature.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated optimization of routing, speed, and heterogeneous fleet composition within a time-dependent PRP, moving beyond single vehicle types or simplified emission models to address the strategic-tactical-operational nexus in green fleet management.

proposals.title: A Column Generation Heuristic for the Fleet Deployment Problem with Stochastic Demand and Time-Dependent Service Requirements
proposals.motivation: In maritime logistics and postal delivery, fleet deployment must balance cost efficiency with reliable service under uncertain demand and strict time windows. Traditional deterministic models are inadequate, and exact methods for stochastic, time-dependent variants are computationally prohibitive for large-scale, real-world instances. This gap limits the ability of operators to make robust planning decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a column generation framework, dynamically generating routes within a set covering formulation, can effectively solve large-scale stochastic fleet deployment problems by decomposing the complex integer program and incorporating uncertainty and time-dependence directly into the column generation subproblems.
proposals.method: The core will be a set covering master problem solved via branch-and-price. The pricing subproblem will be a resource-constrained elementary shortest path problem, extended to handle stochastic demand via recourse actions or robust optimization techniques, and time-dependent travel times or profits. We will develop a tailored dynamic programming labeling algorithm with dominance rules for the subproblem, embedding techniques from stochastic and time-dependent routing. The overall heuristic will integrate this within a restricted master problem and rounding procedure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable solution approach for a class of complex fleet deployment problems under uncertainty. It will advance methodological integration by combining column generation with stochastic and time-dependent routing models. The resulting algorithm will be validated on benchmark instances derived from maritime and postal logistics, demonstrating practical utility.
proposals.novelty: While column generation is established for vehicle routing, its tailored integration into a unified framework that simultaneously handles stochastic demand *and* time-dependent service constraints for fleet deployment is novel. The specific design of the pricing subproblem to encapsulate these combined complexities represents a significant algorithmic challenge beyond standard applications.

proposals.title: A Multi-Agent Negotiation Framework for Fleet Routing to Balance Emissions and Operational Costs
proposals.motivation: Urban logistics and postal delivery systems face increasing pressure to reduce greenhouse gas emissions while maintaining cost-effective service. Traditional centralized fleet routing models often struggle to incorporate dynamic, vehicle-specific constraints and real-time negotiation for system-wide objectives like emissions. Pre-2026, multi-agent systems have shown promise in distributed logistics, but their application to explicitly trade off environmental and economic costs in vehicle routing is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling vehicles as autonomous agents capable of negotiating route exchanges and task allocations through a structured protocol will yield routing solutions that achieve a superior Pareto-optimal balance between total system emissions and total operational cost compared to centralized, single-objective optimization.
proposals.method: We will model the problem as a multi-agent system where each vehicle is a self-interested agent. A negotiation protocol based on contract-net and alternating-offers bargaining will be developed. Agents will evaluate proposed route swaps using a multi-attribute utility function combining individual cost (time, distance) and estimated emissions (using pre-2026 emission models like the MEET or COPERT factors). The system will be integrated within a metaheuristic framework (e.g., a large neighborhood search) where negotiation cycles act as a cooperative improvement mechanism. Computational experiments will be conducted on benchmark VRP instances adapted with emission parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical negotiation framework for eco-efficient fleet management. It will provide new insights into the trade-offs between environmental and economic objectives in distributed routing. The methodology will extend the application of agent-based systems in operational research, offering a flexible tool for logistics planners in postal and delivery services.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of emission metrics into a decentralized, negotiation-based routing mechanism, moving beyond cost-only agent models. This combines Laporte's expertise in time-dependent and selective routing problems with multi-agent negotiation, a less common approach in fleet management pre-2026.

ideas.title: A Systematic Taxonomy and Gap Analysis for Multi-Depot, Dynamic Network Logistics Problems
ideas.description: This research direction develops a comprehensive taxonomy specifically designed for the complex solution space of logistics and routing problems involving multiple depots and dynamic network elements, such as mobile depots or changing infrastructure. It then applies this taxonomy to conduct a systematic, large-scale analysis of the existing literature to map the current state of research. The core outcome is a precise identification of knowledge gaps, ranked by potential impact and difficulty, revealing which combinations of multi-depot strategies and dynamic network types are most theoretically and practically underserved. This provides a foundational roadmap for the field, directing computational and empirical efforts towards the most synergistic and high-value extensions of classical single-depot, static network models.

ideas.title: Balanced Fleet Utilization: Maximizing Serviced Demand Subject to Quality-of-Service Guarantees
ideas.description: This research develops a robust optimization model for fixed fleet operations that seeks to maximize the volume of serviced demand while enforcing hard or soft constraints on service quality. The model explicitly accounts for customer wait times, adherence to promised time windows, and equitable distribution of workload across the fleet. The output is a set of Pareto-efficient solutions or a single optimized plan that provides a practical balance between throughput and reliability, ensuring the operational solution is viable in real-world settings where customer retention depends on consistent service quality.

ideas.title: Global Multi-Echelon Supply Chain Routing with Emissions as the Primary Objective
ideas.description: This research develops a novel optimization and simulation model for global supply chain routing that inverts the traditional objective, making the minimization of total greenhouse gas emissions and other environmental impacts (e.g., water use, pollution) the primary goal. It scales this objective across the fully integrated, multi-echelon network—from raw material extraction and production to final distribution—capturing complex interdependencies and trade-offs between transportation modes, manufacturing locations, and inventory policies. The core innovation is a system-optimal approach that can identify holistic reconfigurations (e.g., strategic nearshoring, modal shifts, inventory positioning) which would be suboptimal or invisible under a cost-primary model but are essential for deep decarbonization. The outcome is a decision-support tool capable of generating Pareto-efficient frontiers for global supply chain design that prioritize planetary boundaries alongside economic viability.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Echelon Global Supply Chain Routing Under Endogenous Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops a novel robust optimization model for integrated routing across the multiple echelons of a global supply chain. It adapts advanced robust techniques from finance to handle correlated uncertainties in demand and transit times that cascade through the network. The key innovation is modeling how routing decisions at one echelon (e.g., factory shipments) endogenously shape the uncertainty set for the next echelon (e.g., distribution center fulfillment), creating a coordinated defensive strategy. The outcome is a computationally tractable framework for generating global routes that are inherently resilient, maintaining service levels and cost efficiency despite deep, interconnected disruptions.

ideas.title: Learning-augmented column generation for complex VRPs via hybrid heuristic-exact pricing
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid column generation system designed to overcome the computational bottleneck of exact pricing in complex Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs). It integrates a machine learning model, trained as a heuristic pricing oracle, to propose promising columns rapidly, while maintaining algorithmic robustness through fallback to traditional methods. This approach ensures the scalability of branch-and-price to VRP variants with rich constraints that mirror real-world logistics. The result is a practical, accelerated optimization framework that retains convergence guarantees while making industrial-scale complex VRP optimization tractable.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop, Data-Assimilative Combinatorial Optimization for Robust Green Routing
ideas.description: This research designs and validates a next-generation green routing system that closes the loop between combinatorial optimization and real-world operation. It develops a dynamic optimization framework that assimilates live telemetry and sensor data to continuously calibrate its environmental impact models, moving beyond static cost assumptions. The core innovation is the integration of adaptive learning and uncertainty-aware robust/stochastic programming to produce routes and schedules that maintain their eco-efficiency under unpredictable conditions. The outcome is a principled methodology for generating robust, real-world-effective green logistics plans that demonstrably bridge the simulation-to-reality gap.

ideas.title: Multi-Echelon Supply Chain Routing via Metabolic Network Flux Optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts the Constraint-Based Reconstruction and Analysis (COBRA) method from systems biology to the problem of integrated routing across multiple echelons of a global supply chain. In COBRA, a cell's metabolism is modeled as a network, and linear programming is used to compute optimal flux distributions of metabolites to achieve an objective (e.g., growth). Analogously, we propose to reconstruct a global supply chain as a directed network of material and financial flows. The solution involves formulating a large-scale linear (or mixed-integer) programming model where the objective is to maximize total system profit or service level, constrained by capacities, costs, tariffs, and sustainability limits across all interconnected echelons. This approach provides a holistic, computable framework for optimizing routing decisions from raw material to end consumer, treating the entire supply chain as a single, constrained flow system.

ideas.title: Dynamic Coalition Formation for Multi-Depot Vehicle Routing with Mobile Resources
ideas.description: This research synthesizes dynamic multi-depot networks with cooperative game theory to model collaborative vehicle routing where depots and other key resources (e.g., charging stations, swap bodies) are mobile and the network topology changes over time. It applies concepts from dynamic and stochastic cooperative games to solve the dual problem of optimal coalition formation among logistics firms and the optimal spatio-temporal positioning of shared mobile infrastructure. The core challenge is developing cost/allocation rules that are stable over time, ensuring long-term coalitional fairness despite the shifting network and the evolving marginal contributions of each partner's mobile assets.

ideas.title: Adapting Shapley Value Fairness from Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning to Collaborative VRP Cost Allocation
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogous solution from Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) to the original problem of cost allocation in collaborative VRPs. In MARL, the Shapley Value is used to fairly attribute a global reward to individual agents based on their marginal contributions to various coalitions, preventing free-riding. Adapted to the logistics context, we propose a computational framework that uses the Shapley Value to calculate each logistics partner's marginal contribution to cost savings across all possible subgroup collaborations within a larger coalition. This provides a fair, stable, and incentive-compatible method for allocating costs or distributing savings in collaborative vehicle routing, directly translating a fairness mechanism from distributed AI to the field of supply chain optimization.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Inverse Policy Design for Sustainable Logistics
ideas.description: This research develops a novel evolutionary inverse design framework for environmental policy in transportation. It uses multi-objective evolutionary algorithms to generate a diverse Pareto front of efficient vehicle routes balancing cost and emissions. The key innovation is then inverting these optimal routing solutions to identify the specific policy parameters (e.g., carbon tax rates, low-emission zone boundaries, subsidy levels) that would make these solutions economically viable and desirable for logistics operators. This creates a data-driven, iterative feedback loop where policy design is directly informed by the operational trade-offs and solution landscapes discovered through evolutionary computation, moving beyond static analysis to dynamic policy optimization.

ideas.title: Adapting Distributed Computing's Dynamic Resource Allocation to Multi-Depot Logistics
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the single-depot and static network assumptions in logistics by drawing an analogy from distributed computing's solution to dynamic resource allocation. In cloud computing, systems like Borg or Kubernetes manage workloads across a dynamic, distributed pool of compute nodes, using a central scheduler that treats the cluster as a single, fungible resource pool. It continuously monitors node state and re-assigns tasks in real-time. Adapted to logistics, this inspires a study of multi-depot problems with dynamic network structures (like mobile depots) through a 'logistics scheduler' architecture. This system would view a fleet of mobile depots (trucks, drones, temporary hubs) as a dynamic resource pool, continuously re-optimizing and re-assigning delivery tasks in real-time based on depot location, capacity, traffic, and demand, fundamentally inverting the static, single-depot planning paradigm.

ideas.title: Emission Minimization within a Viability-Constrained Solution Space
ideas.description: This redesigned approach reframes the problem as finding the minimum possible emissions pathway that still resides within a defined space of economically and operationally viable solutions. Instead of a single objective, it uses a constraint-based model where factors like maximum cost thresholds, minimum service levels, and critical infrastructure resilience form non-negotiable boundaries. The optimization algorithm then exhaustively searches this bounded space for the global emissions minimum, ensuring the solution is both environmentally optimal and practically implementable. This guarantees outcomes that are radical in emissions reduction yet remain within the realm of political and economic feasibility.

ideas.title: A Multi-Physics Inspired Framework for Complex, Multi-Attribute VRPs
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to address rich Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs) with complex, multi-attribute constraints by drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). In CFD, complex multi-physics simulations are tackled not with a single monolithic solver, but through a framework of loosely coupled, specialized solvers (e.g., for fluid dynamics, structural mechanics, heat transfer) coordinated by a master orchestration layer that manages their interactions. We adapt this solution to the VRP context. The adapted idea is to develop a modular optimization framework for VRPs where dedicated solvers or heuristic modules each handle a specific class of realistic industry constraints (such as time windows, multi-dimensional loading, driver hours, green vehicle energy profiles, or client preferences). A central coordination algorithm would then orchestrate these modules, iteratively exchanging partial solutions and constraint violation penalties, to converge on a feasible and high-quality integrated route plan. This approach aims to make the incorporation of diverse, complex constraints more tractable and scalable than attempting to encode them all into a single, increasingly cumbersome optimization model.

ideas.title: Adaptive Receding-Horizon Control for Dynamic VRPs using Model Predictive Control Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the core principle of Model Predictive Control (MPC) from Process Control Engineering to create robust algorithms for dynamic and stochastic Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs). In MPC, a system model is used to solve a finite-horizon optimization problem at each control interval; only the immediate control action is implemented before the horizon recedes and the problem is re-solved with new state information, making it robust to disturbances. The adapted idea is to develop VRP algorithms that operate on a receding horizon: at each decision epoch (e.g., when a vehicle finishes a service), the algorithm solves a VRP sub-problem for a short look-ahead period, incorporating real-time requests, updated traffic conditions, and stochastic forecasts of future demand. This creates a closed-loop, adaptive solution method that commits only to the next actionable steps, continually adjusting the plan in response to dynamic changes and uncertainty during execution.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Partitioning for Heterogeneous Fleet VRP
ideas.description: This idea challenges the homogeneous fleet assumption in VRP by drawing an analogy from community ecology's niche partitioning. In ecology, diverse species coexist by specializing on different resources, reducing competition. The analogous solution is to model a heterogeneous vehicle fleet as an ecological community, where each vehicle type has a 'niche' defined by its operational capabilities and costs. The adapted research idea develops a meta-heuristic optimization framework that partitions the customer demand 'resource space'—considering factors like delivery windows, location clusters, and load types—and matches these niches to the most 'fit' vehicle type. This biologically inspired approach co-optimizes fleet composition and routing to maximize overall system efficiency, akin to how natural communities maximize ecosystem resource use.

ideas.title: Knowledge-Infused, Surrogate-Assisted Multi-Objective Evolutionary Optimization for Cost-Environmental Trade-Offs
ideas.description: This redesigned approach embeds domain expertise and physical constraints directly into the evolutionary search process to overcome the data-inefficiency and knowledge-blindness of standard methods. It employs adaptive surrogate models, trained on both historical data and physics-based simulations, to provide cheap, guided evaluations of candidate solutions. The algorithm's operators are constrained by explicit business rules and environmental regulations, ensuring all explored solutions are actionable. This results in a system that rapidly converges on a diverse, high-quality, and feasible Pareto-optimal set for cost-environmental objectives, making it practical for real-world deployment.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Energy Minimization to Green Vehicle Routing
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from computational biology. The analogous problem is protein folding, where combinatorial optimization tools are used to find the molecular conformation with the minimum free energy. The solution involves coarse-grained modeling and hybrid algorithms that balance global search with local refinement. Adapted to green routing, the research would model vehicle routes as conformations and their environmental impact (e.g., fuel consumption, emissions) as a complex 'energy' function to minimize. The proposed solution is to develop a novel optimization framework that first uses a simplified, coarse-grained network model to explore efficient route portfolios broadly, then employs a detailed local search—incorporating real-time traffic, vehicle dynamics, and precise emission models—to fine-tune schedules and driving patterns, thereby finding a globally optimal 'green' routing solution.

ideas.title: Online Adaptive Distributionally Robust Optimization for Routing Under Uncertainty
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond static robust optimization by creating a closed-loop system that learns the nature of uncertainty from data. It uses online learning algorithms to dynamically update an ambiguity set describing plausible probability distributions of travel times and demands. The core routing engine then solves a distributionally robust optimization model over this adaptive ambiguity set, balancing robustness with adaptability. This results in routes that are not only protective against uncertainty but also evolve to avoid excessive conservatism as more real-world data is gathered.

ideas.title: Apply Multi-Objective Neuroevolution for Supply Chain Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Neuroevolution of Augmenting Topologies (NEAT) algorithm from computational neuroscience to the domain of sustainable supply chain design. The analogous problem in NEAT is evolving neural network structures that trade off performance against complexity. Here, we adapt that solution to evolve physical supply chain network structures, where the competing objectives are economic cost and environmental impact. The algorithm would start with simple supply chain models and complexify them through evolutionary operations (adding a recycling facility, altering a transport route), using speciation to protect innovative structural changes and a multi-objective optimization to map the Pareto frontier of designs balancing cost and sustainability goals.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale, Hierarchical Decomposition Algorithm for Very Large-Scale VRPs
ideas.description: This research adapts solution strategies from computational cosmology's N-body problem to solve very large-scale Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs) with thousands of customers. The analogous problem—calculating gravitational forces in a system of billions of bodies—is solved using hierarchical tree structures and adaptive multi-scale methods to avoid evaluating all pairwise interactions. The adapted idea is to design a hierarchical spatial decomposition algorithm for VRP. It will recursively partition the customer set into a tree of clusters, use coarse-grained routing between cluster representatives, and perform detailed optimization only within local clusters, while approximating the costs of traversing between distant clusters. This multi-scale approach aims to reduce the effective problem size at each step, enabling the efficient handling of previously intractable, continent-scale VRPs.

ideas.title: Integrated Bayesian Learning and Adaptive Routing for Demand-Unknown VRPs
ideas.description: This system solves VRPs where customer demand distributions are initially unknown or poorly specified. It combines online Bayesian learning with adaptive re-optimization, allowing the vehicle to simultaneously learn demand patterns from observed deliveries and adjust its route in real-time. The approach explicitly trades off the cost of exploratory detours against the long-term benefit of reduced uncertainty, ensuring robust performance even with minimal prior data. This creates a practical, closed-loop solution applicable to new service areas or volatile demand environments.

ideas.title: Adaptive Packet Routing for Dynamic Vehicle Routing Problems
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the static assumption in environmental vehicle routing by investigating dynamic, real-time versions of the problem. Drawing an analogy from computer networking, where adaptive packet routing protocols (e.g., OSPF) solve the problem of dynamic data flow by continuously monitoring network state and making distributed routing decisions. The adapted idea is to develop a dynamic routing framework for vehicles, where they receive live updates on environmental and traffic conditions. Inspired by packet routing, vehicles would use a distributed protocol to make localized re-routing choices in real-time, aiming to optimize system-wide environmental metrics like total energy use or emissions, thereby creating a resilient and adaptive green logistics system.

ideas.title: Robust Micro-Vehicle Routing via Integrated Offline Planning and Real-Time Adaptive Control
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid framework for micro-vehicle routing that combines an offline, constraint-aware Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) solver with a decentralized, real-time adaptive control system. The offline planner generates an optimal nominal schedule and route assignment considering payload, range, and fleet constraints. During execution, each vehicle uses a local model-predictive controller to dynamically adjust its speed, path, and energy consumption in response to real-time disturbances like weather, obstacles, and communication delays, while coordinating with the fleet to maintain global mission objectives. The system is validated through high-fidelity simulation and hardware-in-the-loop testing with micro-drones, measuring robustness metrics such as mission completion rate and energy efficiency under uncertainty.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Echelon Routing via Adversarial Scenario Ensemble Optimization
ideas.description: This research develops a global supply chain routing model that integrates stochastic optimization with an adversarial scenario generator. The core system is trained not on a single forecast but on a dynamic ensemble of plausible high-impact disruption scenarios, including geopolitical, climatic, and logistical shocks. The optimization objective shifts from minimizing expected cost to maximizing the robustness of service levels across this adversarial scenario set, producing routing policies with built-in contingency adaptation. The final output is a decision-support framework that prescribes primary routes alongside pre-computed, context-sensitive rerouting protocols for different disruption classes.

ideas.title: Learning to Decompose and Solve: ML-Guided Hierarchical Optimization for Massive-Scale Vehicle Routing
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel hybrid framework that integrates machine learning with hierarchical optimization to solve vehicle routing problems (VRPs) with tens of thousands of customers. The core synergy is using ML models not to predict a final solution directly, but to learn how to intelligently and adaptively decompose the massive instance into coherent, smaller clusters based on instance features, spatial structure, and constraints. Subsequently, a second tier of ML models, trained on the characteristics of these generated sub-problems, predicts high-quality initial solutions or specialized algorithm configurations for solving each cluster efficiently. This creates a feedback loop where the decomposition and solution prediction models are co-trained, enabling the system to automatically discover and exploit recurring problem structures at scale, dramatically surpassing the capabilities of static decomposition schemes or pure ML solvers.

ideas.title: Identifiable Policy Inference via Multi-Objective Inverse Optimization of Transport Networks
ideas.description: This research develops a method to infer robust environmental policy parameters from observed vehicle routing behavior by treating it as a partially observable inverse optimization problem. It uses an ensemble of routing solutions across different regions and time periods to construct a feasible set of policy parameters that are consistent with the data, explicitly addressing the non-uniqueness issue. The core innovation is a bi-level framework that combines inverse reinforcement learning with identifiability constraints derived from auxiliary economic and behavioral data. The output is not a single policy but a sensitivity analysis and a prioritized set of regulatory interventions most likely to have generated the observed efficient routing patterns, along with confidence metrics.

ideas.title: Distributionally Robust VRP Solution Prediction with Uncertainty Quantification
ideas.description: This research develops a machine learning system that not only predicts promising solution structures for Vehicle Routing Problems but also quantifies the uncertainty of its own predictions and maintains robustness under distribution shifts. The system uses conformal prediction to generate prediction sets or intervals with guaranteed coverage probabilities, indicating when the model's suggestions are likely to be reliable. It is trained using techniques that expose it to a wide range of synthetic or adversarially generated VRP instances to learn domain-invariant features, preventing overfitting to a specific training distribution. This results in a tool that can safely guide heuristic search or parameter tuning, even when faced with novel problem instances, by knowing when to rely on its predictions and when to fall back to classical methods.

ideas.title: Transfer column generation from crew scheduling to complex VRPs via analogy to quantum chemistry's configuration interaction
ideas.description: This idea expands the transfer of column generation from crew scheduling to complex VRPs by drawing an analogy from quantum chemistry's Configuration Interaction (CI) method. In CI, an intractable quantum problem is solved by iteratively adding the most significant 'excited-state' configurations to a reference solution. Analogously, for complex VRPs with vast route possibilities, we propose using a meta-heuristic solution as an initial 'reference routing'. The column generation subproblem is then guided to preferentially generate and price new columns (routes) that are intelligent perturbations or 'excitations' of this reference set—routes that modify a few edges or sequences—rather than exploring the entire route space blindly. This adapts the CI philosophy of targeted, importance-guided expansion to make the Branch-and-Price algorithm for VRPs more efficient and scalable, focusing computational effort on columns most likely to enter the optimal basis.

ideas.title: Behaviorally-Grounded Multi-Agent Digital Twin for Urban Green Logistics Policy Design
ideas.description: This research develops a high-fidelity digital twin of a city's logistics ecosystem, populated by autonomous software agents representing carriers, drivers, and distribution centers with calibrated behavioral models. The system employs federated learning to securely integrate real operational data from private companies, ensuring agent behaviors reflect actual cost structures and decision-making heuristics. It is designed to simulate and stress-test the rollout of environmental regulations (like low-emission zones or congestion pricing) and incentive schemes, predicting emergent network outcomes, compliance rates, and unintended consequences. The output is a robust policy and coordination toolkit that identifies the minimal, targeted interventions needed to steer the self-interested actions of independent actors toward the city-wide environmental optimum.

ideas.title: Emissions-First Optimization via Thermodynamic Efficiency Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the thermodynamic approach of making efficiency the primary objective (as in Carnot cycle analysis) to environmental systems. Instead of minimizing cost with emissions constraints, we establish minimum theoretical emissions as the fundamental optimization target, analogous to maximum theoretical efficiency in heat engines. This emissions-first paradigm would restructure optimization algorithms across domains like supply chains, manufacturing, and energy systems, treating emissions as the core objective to minimize while treating cost as a secondary constraint, potentially revealing novel system configurations and trade-offs invisible to cost-first optimization models.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Policy Landscapes from Agent Trajectories
ideas.description: This research adapts the core solution from inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) in computational neuroscience to the domain of environmental policy design. The analogous IRL problem—inferring an agent's reward function from its optimal trajectories—is translated into analyzing large-scale, real-world efficient vehicle routing solutions to deduce the implicit cost structures drivers respond to. The adapted idea proposes an inverse design framework where these inferred cost structures are used to calibrate and optimize explicit environmental policy parameters (e.g., zoning, pricing, access rules), thereby ensuring that individually efficient routes collectively scale to meet system-level environmental targets, addressing the SCALING_DUAL dimension of the original seed.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map and Optimize Green Vehicle Routing Corridors
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution of Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) from conservation biology to the domain of green vehicle routing. In ecology, ENM correlates species presence/absence data with environmental variables to create a spatial suitability map, guiding conservation corridor design. The analogous solution is adapted by treating vehicles as the 'species' and their environmental impact (e.g., emissions, noise, energy use) as the 'fitness' metric. The research would involve developing a methodology to build a 'Green Routing Suitability Surface' by analyzing historical telematics and emissions data against variables like traffic flow, road infrastructure, topography, and regulatory zones (e.g., low-emission areas). This geospatial layer would function as a foundational environmental constraint map, enabling routing algorithms to optimize paths not just for distance or time, but for minimal environmental 'unsuitability,' effectively creating prioritized green corridors within the transportation network. The outcome would be a systematic framework for integrating complex, multi-dimensional environmental constraints into routing problems via an ecologically-inspired, data-driven mapping approach.

ideas.title: A Phylogenetic Taxonomy for Environmental Vehicle Routing Problems
ideas.description: This research proposes developing a taxonomy for vehicle routing problems with environmental issues using an analogy from evolutionary biology's phylogenetic systematics. The analogous solution involves classifying organisms based on shared derived characteristics and evolutionary history to create a cladistic taxonomy. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to construct a phylogenetic taxonomy for Environmental Vehicle Routing Problems (EVRPs). This method would group problem types into a tree structure based on their shared, fundamental environmental constraints—such as the primary energy source (electric, hybrid, conventional), the nature of the externalities modeled (CO2, noise, congestion), or the regulatory mechanism (carbon cap, cost, tax)—and their conceptual descent from classic VRP formulations. This approach moves beyond a flat feature list to reveal the 'evolutionary' relationships and lineages among EVRPs, providing a structured framework that clarifies the field's development and identifies core branching points for future research.

ideas.title: Robust Route Design via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts robust optimization techniques from finance by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, robust Species Distribution Models (SDMs) use ensemble forecasting across multiple climate scenarios to create conservation plans resilient to environmental uncertainty. The analogous solution is adapted by treating routing scenarios (varying demand and travel time predictions) like ecological scenarios. An ensemble of potential routes is generated from different predictive models. A robust consensus mechanism then identifies the most stable and reliable route segments across this ensemble, resulting in a final route design that is immunized against the inherent uncertainty in demand and network conditions, much like a conservation corridor designed to be viable across multiple future climates.

ideas.title: Employ column generation for large-scale environmentally constrained routing via protein folding analogies
ideas.description: This idea applies column generation techniques to solve large-scale environmentally constrained routing problems by drawing an analogy from computational biology's protein folding solutions. In protein structure prediction, the immense space of possible folds is managed by generating and testing promising structural fragments iteratively, rather than through full enumeration. Adapted to routing, the method would involve an iterative column generation algorithm where a 'fragment pool' of promising, short, environmentally-efficient route segments is maintained. A pricing subproblem, informed by the master problem's dual variables and localized environmental constraints (like emission zones, elevation, or traffic), would generate new candidate fragments (columns) that could be assembled into complete, feasible routes. This approach decomposes the massive route enumeration problem into a more manageable search through a space of high-quality, constraint-compliant fragments, significantly improving scalability for problems with complex, granular environmental constraints.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Adversarial Simulation for Robust Dynamic VRP Algorithms
ideas.description: This research develops dynamic VRP algorithms within a high-fidelity, closed-loop simulation platform that models the reactive and strategic behaviors of all actors in the system (e.g., drivers, customers, competing services). The core methodology involves co-evolving solution algorithms alongside adaptive adversarial models that learn to exploit algorithmic weaknesses, creating non-stationary, feedback-rich environments. This approach ensures robustness is tested and achieved against system-level dynamics and strategic counter-adaptation, not just passive randomness. The outcome is a suite of algorithms and a simulation framework that prove resilient to the complex, emergent realities of real-world deployment.

ideas.title: Predicting VRP Solutions via Protein Folding-Inspired Neural Networks
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from computational biology's protein structure prediction to the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP). In protein folding, models like AlphaFold2 predict a protein's complex 3D structure from its linear amino acid sequence by learning patterns from known structures. Adapted to VRP, we propose training a deep neural network to predict promising solution structures (e.g., customer clustering patterns, route ordering priors, or effective metaheuristic parameters) directly from the raw instance data. The network would be trained on a large dataset of solved VRP instances, learning to map problem features to solution templates. This predicted 'blueprint' would then guide a conventional solver, drastically reducing its search space and computation time, analogous to how a predicted protein fold provides a starting point for detailed biochemical analysis.

ideas.title: Anomaly-Aware Literature Analysis for Novel Gap Discovery
ideas.description: This system combines structured taxonomic review with an unsupervised anomaly detection pipeline to uncover research gaps. It first applies a standard taxonomy to map the known literature landscape, then uses semantic embeddings and network analysis to identify conceptual outliers and weakly connected research clusters. These anomalies are evaluated for citation dynamics and terminological novelty to distinguish genuine, overlooked opportunities from irrelevant noise. The final output is a dual-layer gap analysis: confirmed gaps within the existing taxonomy and high-potential novel gaps at the field's conceptual frontiers.

ideas.title: Systematically Map Research Gaps via a 'Phylogenetic' Literature Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from evolutionary biology's phylogenetics to the task of identifying research gaps. In phylogenetics, scientists build trees to map species relationships and pinpoint gaps in the fossil record by analyzing branching patterns and trait distributions. Similarly, we propose constructing a 'phylogenetic' map of the literature within a field. Papers are treated as taxa, and their core elements (methods, theories, findings) as heritable traits. Using the proposed taxonomy to code these traits, a network or tree is generated to visualize the intellectual lineage and divergence of ideas. Research gaps are then systematically uncovered as sparse or underdeveloped branches in this map, isolated clusters indicating niche topics, or key conceptual nodes with few subsequent studies, thereby providing a visual and analytical method to direct future work toward the most significant underexplored avenues.
